mcq.q: The site of sperm production in the male reproductive system is the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Seminiferous tubules — coiled tubes inside the testis lined with germinal epithelium where spermatogenesis occurs.

mcq.q: The function of the epididymis is to ...
opts: Store and mature sperm before ejaculation
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The epididymis stores sperm while they mature and gain the ability to swim.

mcq.q: Which gland secretes a fluid rich in fructose that provides energy for sperm?
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. The seminal vesicles secrete a fructose-rich, alkaline fluid that nourishes sperm and forms most of the semen volume.

mcq.q: The function of the prostate gland secretion is to ...
opts: Neutralise the acidity of the vagina and urethra, activating sperm motility
Carry sperm from testis to epididymis
mcq.exp: Correct answer: A. Prostate fluid is alkaline and milky, neutralising vaginal/urethral acidity and increasing sperm motility.

mcq.q: The bulbourethral (Cowper's) gland functions to ...
opts: Secrete a small amount of clear fluid that lubricates and neutralises acidic urine residue in the urethra before ejaculation
Produce the bulk of seminal fluid
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Cowper's gland fluid lubricates the urethra and neutralises any remaining acidic urine.

mcq.q: The vas deferens (ductus deferens) functions to ...
opts: Carry sperm from the epididymis to the urethra during ejaculation
Regulate body temperature of the testes
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The vas deferens is a muscular tube that uses peristalsis to transport mature sperm from the epididymis to the urethra.

mcq.q: The scrotum keeps the testes at a temperature ... than body temperature, which is necessary for ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Sperm production requires a temperature about 2–3 °C below core body temperature; the scrotum (with the cremaster and dartos muscles) regulates this.

mcq.q: The dartos and cremaster muscles in the scrotum function to ...
opts: Contract or relax to adjust the position of the testes and regulate their temperature
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. These muscles move the testes closer to or further from the body to maintain optimal temperature for spermatogenesis.

mcq.q: Interstitial (Leydig) cells, found between the seminiferous tubules, are responsible for ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Leydig cells secrete testosterone in response to Luteinising Hormone (LH/ICSH).

mcq.q: Sertoli (nurse) cells within the seminiferous tubules function to ...
opts: Nourish and support developing sperm cells and form the blood-testis barrier
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Sertoli cells support, nourish and protect developing spermatids and regulate the environment within the seminiferous tubule.

mcq.q: The penis becomes erect due to ...
opts: Engorgement of erectile tissue (corpora cavernosa and corpus spongiosum) with blood
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. During sexual arousal, arteries dilate and erectile tissue fills with blood, causing an erection.

mcq.q: Semen is composed mainly of secretions from the ...
opts: Seminal vesicles, prostate gland and bulbourethral glands, plus sperm from the testes
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Semen = sperm (testes) + fluids from seminal vesicles (~60%), prostate (~30%) and bulbourethral glands.

mcq.q: Testosterone is responsible for all the following EXCEPT ...
opts: Development of secondary male sexual characteristics
Stimulating the development of the corpus luteum
Development of male reproductive organs before birth
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. The corpus luteum is a female structure formed from a ruptured follicle; testosterone is a male hormone and plays no role in its development.

mcq.q: The site of egg cell (ovum) production is the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The ovaries contain follicles in which oogenesis takes place.

mcq.q: Fertilisation normally takes place in the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. The sperm meets the egg in the upper third (ampulla) of the fallopian tube.

mcq.q: The function of the fimbriae is to ...
opts: Sweep the released ovum into the fallopian tube
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Finger-like fimbriae at the end of the fallopian tube create currents that draw the ovulated egg in.

mcq.q: Cilia lining the fallopian tube function to ...
opts: Move the ovum towards the uterus by wave-like beating
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Ciliary beating, together with peristalsis of the tube wall, moves the ovum/zygote towards the uterus.

mcq.q: The layer of the uterus that thickens with blood vessels each month to prepare for implantation is the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The endometrium is the inner, vascular lining of the uterus that is shed during menstruation if implantation does not occur.

mcq.q: The myometrium is the layer of the uterus responsible for ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The myometrium is the thick smooth muscle layer that contracts powerfully during parturition (birth).

mcq.q: The function of the cervix is to ...
opts: Separate the uterus from the vagina and produce mucus that changes in consistency through the cycle
Carry the ovum to the uterus
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The cervix's mucus becomes thin and watery near ovulation (aiding sperm passage) and thick afterwards (forming a plug).

mcq.q: The vagina serves as ...
opts: The birth canal and receptacle for the penis during intercourse
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The vagina is the muscular, elastic canal connecting the cervix to the exterior of the body.

mcq.q: A primary follicle in the ovary consists of ...
opts: A primary oocyte surrounded by a single layer of follicle cells
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. As the follicle develops it gains additional follicle cell layers and fluid-filled spaces, becoming a Graafian (mature) follicle.

mcq.q: The corpus luteum develops from ...
opts: The remains of the follicle after ovulation
mcq.exp: Correct answer: A. After ovulation, the ruptured follicle is transformed by LH into the corpus luteum, which secretes progesterone (and some oestrogen).

mcq.q: If fertilisation does NOT occur, the corpus luteum ...
opts: Degenerates into the corpus albicans, causing progesterone levels to fall and menstruation to begin
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Without hCG stimulation from an embryo, the corpus luteum degenerates after about 10–14 days.

mcq.q: Spermatogenesis is the process by which ...
opts: Diploid spermatogonia divide by mitosis and meiosis to form haploid spermatids
Sperm gain motility in the epididymis
mcq.exp: Correct answer: A. Spermatogonia (2n) undergo mitosis, then primary and secondary spermatocytes undergo meiosis I and II, ultimately producing four haploid spermatids per spermatogonium.

mcq.q: During spermatogenesis, ONE primary spermatocyte gives rise to ... functional sperm cells.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Meiosis I produces 2 secondary spermatocytes; meiosis II produces 2 spermatids from each, giving 4 spermatids (which differentiate into 4 sperm) in total.

mcq.q: Spermiogenesis refers to ...
opts: The division of spermatogonia by mitosis
The differentiation of spermatids into mature, motile sperm cells
The fusion of sperm and egg
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Spermiogenesis is the maturation/differentiation stage where spermatids develop a tail, acrosome and lose most cytoplasm.

mcq.q: The acrosome of a sperm cell contains ...
opts: Hydrolytic/digestive enzymes used to penetrate the egg's protective layers
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The acrosome cap, derived from the Golgi apparatus, releases enzymes during the acrosome reaction to digest the zona pellucida.

mcq.q: The midpiece of a sperm cell is packed with mitochondria because ...
opts: They provide ATP/energy for the beating of the tail (flagellum)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Mitochondria generate the ATP needed to power the whip-like movement of the flagellum.

mcq.q: Oogenesis differs from spermatogenesis in that ...
opts: It produces 4 functional gametes and 0 polar bodies
It produces only 1 functional ovum and up to 3 polar bodies from one primary oocyte
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Unequal cytoplasmic division during meiosis in oogenesis produces one large functional ovum and small, non-functional polar bodies.

mcq.q: Polar bodies formed during oogenesis ...
opts: Are small, non-functional cells that eventually degenerate
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Polar bodies receive very little cytoplasm and degenerate; they do not develop further.

mcq.q: At birth, a female's ovaries already contain ...
opts: Primary oocytes arrested in prophase I of meiosis
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. All primary oocytes a female will ever have are formed before birth and remain suspended in prophase I until puberty.

mcq.q: Meiosis in oogenesis is only completed (Meiosis II) if ...
opts: Fertilisation by a sperm cell occurs
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The secondary oocyte is arrested in metaphase II at ovulation and only completes meiosis II if penetrated by a sperm cell.

mcq.q: Which of the following is a similarity between spermatogenesis and oogenesis?
opts: Both involve meiosis to halve the chromosome number, producing haploid gametes
Both occur continuously throughout life at the same rate
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Both processes use meiosis I and II to reduce the diploid number to haploid.

mcq.q: Follicle Stimulating Hormone (FSH) is secreted by the ... and stimulates ...
opts: Anterior pituitary; growth/development of follicles in the ovary
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. FSH from the anterior pituitary stimulates follicle development and oestrogen secretion at the start of the cycle.

mcq.q: The sudden surge of LH (Luteinising Hormone) at about day 14 of the cycle directly triggers ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The LH surge causes the mature (Graafian) follicle to rupture, releasing the secondary oocyte — ovulation.

mcq.q: Rising oestrogen levels in the first half of the menstrual cycle cause the ...
opts: Endometrium to thicken (proliferate) and repair after menstruation
Pituitary to stop secreting FSH permanently
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Oestrogen from the developing follicle stimulates regrowth and thickening of the endometrium (proliferative phase).

mcq.q: During the luteal phase, progesterone (secreted mainly by the corpus luteum) functions to ...
opts: Maintain and further thicken the endometrium, preparing it for possible implantation, and inhibit FSH/LH
Cause the cervix mucus to thin
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Progesterone maintains the secretory endometrium and exerts negative feedback on the hypothalamus/pituitary, suppressing FSH and LH.

mcq.q: Menstruation occurs because ...
opts: Progesterone and oestrogen levels fall sharply (due to corpus luteum degeneration), causing the endometrium to break down and be shed
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The drop in progesterone/oestrogen withdraws hormonal support for the endometrium, which breaks down and is shed as the menstrual flow.

mcq.q: The high levels of oestrogen just before ovulation have what effect on FSH and LH secretion?
opts: Negative feedback, decreasing FSH and LH
Positive feedback, causing a surge in FSH and especially LH
They stop the pituitary from functioning
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Unusually, a high, sustained level of oestrogen briefly switches to positive feedback, triggering the LH surge that causes ovulation.

mcq.q: A typical menstrual cycle lasts approximately ... days, with ovulation occurring around day ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The average cycle is about 28 days, with ovulation occurring roughly midway, around day 14.

mcq.q: During pregnancy, FSH and LH levels remain LOW mainly because ...
opts: High levels of oestrogen and progesterone (from the corpus luteum, then placenta) exert negative feedback, preventing further follicle development/ovulation
There is no more GnRH produced ever again
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Sustained high oestrogen/progesterone suppress GnRH, FSH and LH via negative feedback, preventing additional ovulations during pregnancy.

mcq.q: Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) is secreted by the ... and stimulates the ...
opts: Hypothalamus; anterior pituitary to release FSH and LH
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. GnRH from the hypothalamus stimulates the anterior pituitary to secrete FSH and LH.

mcq.q: Which sequence correctly represents events of the ovarian cycle?
opts: Ovulation → follicle development → corpus luteum formation
Follicle development → ovulation → corpus luteum formation → corpus luteum degeneration (if no fertilisation)
Corpus luteum formation → follicle development → ovulation
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. This is the correct chronological order of the ovarian cycle.

mcq.q: Fertilisation is defined as ...
opts: The fusion of the haploid nucleus of the sperm with the haploid nucleus of the secondary oocyte to form a diploid zygote
The release of the egg from the ovary
mcq.exp: Correct answer: A. Fertilisation restores the diploid (2n) chromosome number, forming a zygote.

mcq.q: The acrosome reaction refers to ...
opts: The hardening of the zona pellucida after one sperm has entered (preventing polyspermy)
The release of enzymes from the sperm's acrosome to digest the corona radiata and zona pellucida
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Enzymes released from the acrosome digest a path through the egg's protective layers, allowing the sperm to reach and fuse with the oocyte membrane.

mcq.q: Polyspermy (fertilisation by more than one sperm) is normally prevented by ...
opts: Rapid changes in the egg membrane and hardening of the zona pellucida (cortical reaction) after the first sperm enters
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The cortical reaction releases enzymes that harden the zona pellucida, blocking entry of additional sperm.

mcq.q: Cleavage refers to ...
opts: Growth in the size of the zygote
A series of rapid mitotic divisions of the zygote without an increase in overall size, forming a morula then blastocyst
Meiotic division of the secondary oocyte
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Cleavage divisions increase cell number but the embryo's total volume stays roughly the same until later growth.

mcq.q: The blastocyst stage of development is characterised by ...
opts: A solid ball of identical cells
A hollow, fluid-filled ball of cells with an inner cell mass that will become the embryo and an outer trophoblast layer
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The trophoblast later forms the placenta/membranes; the inner cell mass develops into the embryo.

mcq.q: Implantation occurs when the blastocyst embeds itself in the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The blastocyst burrows into the thickened, vascular endometrium, usually about 6–10 days after fertilisation.

mcq.q: Identical (monozygotic) twins form when ...
opts: Two separate eggs are fertilised by two separate sperm
One zygote splits into two separate embryos early in development
One egg is fertilised by two sperm
Two different sperm fertilise the same egg
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Monozygotic twins arise from a single fertilised egg that splits, so they share identical genetic material.

mcq.q: Fraternal (dizygotic) twins form when ...
opts: A single zygote splits into two
Two separate eggs are released and fertilised by two separate sperm in the same cycle
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Dizygotic twins develop from two different zygotes and are genetically as similar as any siblings.

mcq.q: An ectopic pregnancy occurs when implantation takes place ...
opts: Outside the uterus, most commonly in the fallopian tube
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Ectopic pregnancies, most often in the fallopian tube, are dangerous as the tube cannot support a growing embryo.

mcq.q: The placenta develops mainly from the ...
opts: Chorion (trophoblast-derived tissue) of the embryo together with the endometrium of the uterus
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The placenta is a composite structure formed from foetal (chorionic villi) and maternal (endometrial) tissue.

mcq.q: Which of the following does NOT cross the placental barrier under normal circumstances?
opts: Maternal and foetal blood mixing directly
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Maternal and foetal blood do NOT mix; exchange occurs across the placental membrane by diffusion/active transport, keeping the two blood supplies separate.

mcq.q: A key function of the placenta is to ...
opts: Allow exchange of gases, nutrients and waste between mother and foetus, and secrete hormones to maintain pregnancy
Replace the function of the uterus
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The placenta exchanges O2/CO2, nutrients/waste, and secretes hCG, oestrogen and progesterone.

mcq.q: Human Chorionic Gonadotropin (hCG) is significant because it ...
opts: Maintains the corpus luteum early in pregnancy, preventing menstruation
mcq.exp: Correct answer: A. hCG, secreted by the developing embryo/chorion, keeps the corpus luteum active so it continues producing progesterone; it is the hormone detected in pregnancy tests.

mcq.q: The umbilical cord connects the foetus to the placenta and contains ...
opts: Blood vessels (umbilical arteries and vein) carrying blood between foetus and placenta
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The umbilical cord carries deoxygenated blood/waste to the placenta and oxygenated/nutrient-rich blood back to the foetus.

mcq.q: The amniotic sac and fluid function to ...
opts: Cushion and protect the foetus from physical shock and maintain a stable temperature
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Amniotic fluid acts as a shock absorber and helps regulate temperature, also allowing foetal movement.

mcq.q: Towards the end of pregnancy, the placenta takes over hormone production from the corpus luteum, secreting mainly ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. By about 12 weeks, the placenta becomes the main source of oestrogen and progesterone, maintaining pregnancy.

mcq.q: Oxytocin, released by the posterior pituitary, functions during birth to ...
opts: Stimulate strong contractions of the myometrium (positive feedback)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Oxytocin causes uterine contractions that intensify in a positive feedback loop as labour progresses (also stimulates milk let-down after birth).

mcq.q: Relaxin, secreted during late pregnancy, functions to ...
opts: Relax/loosen the pelvic ligaments and soften/dilate the cervix in preparation for birth
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Relaxin prepares the body physically for childbirth by increasing the flexibility of the pelvis and cervix.

mcq.q: The three stages of labour, in correct order, are ...
opts: Delivery of placenta → dilation of cervix → delivery of baby
Dilation of the cervix → delivery of the baby → delivery of the placenta (afterbirth)
Delivery of the baby → dilation of cervix → delivery of placenta
Rupture of membranes → delivery of placenta → dilation
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Stage 1: cervical dilation; Stage 2: delivery of the baby; Stage 3: delivery of the placenta.

mcq.q: The combined oral contraceptive pill prevents pregnancy mainly by ...
opts: Containing synthetic oestrogen and progesterone that inhibit FSH/LH secretion, preventing ovulation
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Steady high hormone levels suppress GnRH/FSH/LH via negative feedback, so no follicle matures and ovulation does not occur.

mcq.q: A condom is an example of a ... method of contraception.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Condoms physically prevent sperm from reaching the egg and are also the only contraceptive that protects against STIs.

mcq.q: An Intra-Uterine Device (IUD) prevents pregnancy mainly by ...
opts: Stopping ovulation completely in all cases
Creating a hostile environment in the uterus that prevents implantation/fertilisation
Acting as a barrier at the vaginal opening
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. An IUD (copper or hormonal) alters the uterine environment, often preventing fertilisation and implantation.

mcq.q: Sterilisation in males (vasectomy) involves ...
opts: Cutting and sealing the vas deferens so sperm cannot reach the urethra/semen
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. A vasectomy prevents sperm from being included in the ejaculate, while testosterone production and libido are unaffected.

mcq.q: Tubal ligation (female sterilisation) involves ...
opts: Cutting/tying the fallopian tubes so the egg cannot meet sperm and travel to the uterus
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Tubal ligation blocks the fallopian tubes, preventing fertilisation, while ovulation and hormone production continue normally.

mcq.q: The rhythm/calendar (natural) method of contraception relies on ...
opts: Avoiding intercourse during the woman's fertile period, estimated from her menstrual cycle
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. This method has a relatively high failure rate as cycle length and ovulation timing can vary.

mcq.q: The emergency contraceptive pill ('morning-after pill') works mainly by ...
opts: Delaying or preventing ovulation if taken soon after unprotected intercourse
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. It is most effective when taken as soon as possible after intercourse and works primarily by delaying/preventing ovulation.

mcq.q: Which contraceptive method offers protection against both pregnancy AND sexually transmitted infections?
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Condoms create a physical barrier that also blocks the transmission of pathogens, unlike hormonal or surgical methods.

mcq.q: HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus) primarily attacks and destroys ...
opts: CD4 / T-helper lymphocytes (white blood cells), weakening the immune system
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. HIV targets T-helper (CD4) cells, progressively destroying the immune system's ability to fight infection.

mcq.q: AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome) is best described as ...
opts: The advanced stage of HIV infection where the immune system is severely compromised, leading to opportunistic infections
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. AIDS develops when HIV has destroyed enough immune cells that the body can no longer fight off opportunistic infections and certain cancers.

mcq.q: HIV can be transmitted through all of the following EXCEPT ...
opts: Mother-to-child during pregnancy, birth or breastfeeding
Casual contact such as shaking hands or sharing a meal
mcq.exp: Correct answer: D. HIV is NOT spread through casual everyday contact; it requires exchange of specific bodily fluids (blood, semen, vaginal fluid, breast milk).

mcq.q: The most effective way to prevent sexual transmission of HIV/STIs is ...
opts: Correct and consistent use of condoms, and/or abstinence
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Condoms form a physical barrier to bodily fluids; abstinence eliminates risk entirely. Other contraceptives do not protect against infection.

mcq.q: Antiretroviral (ARV) treatment for HIV functions to ...
opts: Suppress viral replication, allowing the immune system to recover and the person to live a long, healthy life
Prevent all forms of contraception from working
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. ARVs do not cure HIV but reduce the viral load dramatically, slowing disease progression.

mcq.q: Gonorrhoea and syphilis are examples of ...
opts: Bacterial STIs that can usually be cured with antibiotics if treated early
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Unlike HIV (a virus), gonorrhoea and syphilis are caused by bacteria and are generally curable with appropriate antibiotic treatment.

mcq.q: Prevention of mother-to-child transmission (PMTCT) of HIV can include ...
opts: ARV treatment for the mother during pregnancy, safe delivery practices, and use of formula or ARV-covered breastfeeding
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. With ARV treatment and appropriate care, the risk of transmitting HIV from mother to child can be greatly reduced.

mcq.q: In-vitro fertilisation (IVF) involves ...
opts: Fertilisation occurring naturally inside the fallopian tube
Eggs and sperm being combined outside the body in a laboratory, with resulting embryo(s) later implanted in the uterus
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. IVF is a fertility treatment used when natural conception is difficult, e.g. due to blocked fallopian tubes.

mcq.q: Artificial insemination involves ...
opts: Surgically placing an embryo in the uterus
Introducing collected sperm directly into the female reproductive tract using instruments, bypassing intercourse
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. This technique is used to assist conception when natural insemination is not possible or successful.

mcq.q: Menopause is best defined as ...
opts: The first menstrual period in a girl's life
The permanent cessation of menstrual cycles and fertility in women, usually due to declining ovarian function
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Menopause typically occurs around age 45–55, as the ovaries stop releasing eggs and oestrogen/progesterone levels decline.

mcq.q: Common symptoms associated with menopause include ...
opts: Hot flushes, mood changes and irregular periods due to fluctuating/declining hormone levels
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Declining and fluctuating oestrogen levels are responsible for most menopausal symptoms.

mcq.q: Surrogacy refers to a situation where ...
opts: A woman carries and delivers a baby for another person/couple, often using IVF
mcq.exp: Correct answer: A. Surrogacy is used when a woman is medically unable to carry a pregnancy herself.

practice.q: Name the structure in the male reproductive system where sperm cells are produced and explain ONE adaptation of this structure for its function.
practice.memo:
• Structure: Seminiferous tubules (in the testis) ✓
• Adaptation: Highly coiled/long, increasing surface area for sperm production; OR lined with germinal epithelium that undergoes meiosis to form sperm ✓✓

practice.q: Explain why the testes are located outside the body cavity, in the scrotum.
practice.memo:
• Sperm production requires a temperature 2–3 °C lower than normal body temperature ✓
• The scrotum, being outside the body, keeps the testes cooler ✓
• This allows normal/optimal spermatogenesis to occur ✓

practice.q: Describe the pathway of sperm cells from the site of production to the point at which they leave the body, naming FOUR structures.
practice.memo: • Seminiferous tubules → Epididymis → Vas deferens (ductus deferens) → Urethra ✓✓✓✓ (1 mark per correct structure in order)

practice.q: Differentiate between the functions of the seminal vesicles and the prostate gland.
practice.memo:
• Seminal vesicles: secrete a fructose-rich fluid that provides energy for sperm motility ✓✓
• Prostate gland: secretes an alkaline fluid that neutralises vaginal/urethral acidity, increasing sperm motility and survival ✓✓

practice.q: Explain the role of testosterone in the male reproductive system.
practice.memo:
• Stimulates and maintains spermatogenesis ✓
• Responsible for the development of secondary sexual characteristics (e.g. deepening voice, facial hair, muscle growth) ✓
• Stimulates development of male reproductive organs ✓
• Maintains libido (sex drive) ✓ (any 4)

practice.q: Name TWO functions of the fallopian tube (oviduct).
practice.memo:
• Site of fertilisation ✓
• Transports the ovum/zygote to the uterus (via cilia and peristalsis) ✓

practice.q: Distinguish between the endometrium and the myometrium in terms of structure and function.
practice.memo:
• Endometrium: inner, glandular/vascular lining; thickens to receive a fertilised egg and is shed during menstruation if no implantation occurs ✓✓
• Myometrium: thick, outer muscular layer; contracts strongly during labour to expel the baby ✓✓

practice.q: Explain the role of the corpus luteum if fertilisation occurs.
practice.memo:
• The corpus luteum is maintained (does not degenerate) due to hCG secreted by the developing embryo ✓
• It continues to secrete progesterone (and oestrogen) ✓
• This maintains the endometrium, preventing menstruation, until the placenta takes over hormone production (~12 weeks) ✓

practice.q: Describe the structure and function of a Graafian (mature) follicle.
practice.memo:
• Structure: large fluid-filled follicle containing a secondary oocyte surrounded by follicle cells, near the surface of the ovary ✓✓
• Function: ruptures at ovulation to release the secondary oocyte; secretes oestrogen ✓✓

practice.q: Compare spermatogenesis and oogenesis in terms of the number of functional gametes produced from one primary sex cell.
practice.memo:
• Spermatogenesis: 4 functional sperm cells from 1 primary spermatocyte ✓
• Oogenesis: 1 functional ovum (+ up to 3 polar bodies, which are non-functional) from 1 primary oocyte ✓

practice.q: Describe THREE structural adaptations of a sperm cell to its function.
practice.memo:
• Acrosome containing enzymes to digest the egg's covering ✓
• Numerous mitochondria in the midpiece to provide energy/ATP for movement ✓
• Flagellum (tail) for swimming/motility ✓
• Streamlined head shape to reduce resistance ✓ (any 3)

practice.q: Explain why a primary oocyte is described as being 'arrested' before birth, and state when it resumes development.
practice.memo:
• Primary oocytes are formed before birth and remain suspended in prophase I of meiosis I ✓
• Development resumes (one oocyte per cycle) from puberty onwards, stimulated by FSH ✓
• Meiosis II is only completed upon fertilisation by a sperm ✓

practice.q: Explain the role of FSH in the menstrual cycle.
practice.memo:
• Secreted by the anterior pituitary ✓
• Stimulates the development/maturation of follicles in the ovary ✓
• Stimulates the follicle to secrete oestrogen ✓

practice.q: Describe the hormonal events that lead to ovulation.
practice.memo:
• FSH stimulates follicle development, which secretes increasing amounts of oestrogen ✓✓
• High oestrogen levels switch from negative to positive feedback on the hypothalamus/pituitary ✓
• This causes a surge in LH (and FSH) ✓
• The LH surge causes the mature follicle to rupture, releasing the secondary oocyte = ovulation ✓✓

practice.q: Explain why menstruation occurs if fertilisation does not take place.
practice.memo:
• No embryo forms, so no hCG is produced ✓
• The corpus luteum is therefore not maintained and degenerates (becomes corpus albicans) ✓
• Progesterone and oestrogen levels fall sharply ✓
• The endometrium loses hormonal support, breaks down and is shed as menstrual flow ✓

practice.q: Using your knowledge of negative feedback, explain how progesterone prevents further ovulation during the luteal phase.
practice.memo:
• High progesterone (with oestrogen) inhibits the hypothalamus from releasing GnRH ✓
• This reduces FSH and LH secretion by the pituitary ✓
• Low FSH/LH prevents further follicle development and ovulation ✓

practice.q: Draw a conclusion about the relationship between LH levels and ovulation, based on hormone graphs typically given in exams.
practice.memo:
• A sharp surge/peak in LH occurs immediately before ovulation ✓
• This LH surge directly triggers/causes ovulation (rupture of the follicle) ✓
• Therefore LH level is a useful indicator for predicting ovulation timing ✓

practice.q: Describe the process of fertilisation, starting from when a sperm reaches the secondary oocyte.
practice.memo:
• Sperm reaches and binds to the corona radiata/zona pellucida surrounding the secondary oocyte ✓
• The acrosome reaction occurs: enzymes are released to digest a path through these layers ✓
• The sperm membrane fuses with the oocyte membrane and the sperm nucleus enters ✓
• This triggers completion of meiosis II in the oocyte (forming a polar body) ✓
• The haploid nuclei of sperm and egg fuse to form a diploid zygote ✓ (any 5)

practice.q: Explain how polyspermy is prevented.
practice.memo:
• Fusion of the first sperm triggers a cortical/membrane reaction ✓
• This causes rapid depolarisation of the egg membrane, then hardening of the zona pellucida ✓
• This prevents any further sperm from penetrating the egg ✓

practice.q: Distinguish between identical and fraternal twins in terms of their formation and genetic similarity.
practice.memo:
• Identical (monozygotic): one zygote splits into two embryos; genetically identical ✓✓
• Fraternal (dizygotic): two separate eggs fertilised by two separate sperm; genetically different (like ordinary siblings) ✓✓

practice.q: Explain why it is important that maternal and foetal blood do not mix directly in the placenta.
practice.memo:
• Maternal and foetal blood may be of different/incompatible blood groups ✓
• Mixing could cause an immune/Rhesus reaction, harming the foetus ✓
• Diffusion across the placental membrane still allows efficient exchange of substances without mixing the blood itself ✓

practice.q: Discuss the role of hCG in early pregnancy and explain why it forms the basis of pregnancy tests.
practice.memo:
• hCG is secreted by the chorion/developing embryo soon after implantation ✓
• It maintains the corpus luteum, which continues secreting progesterone to maintain the endometrium ✓
• This prevents menstruation, sustaining the pregnancy ✓
• hCG is excreted in the mother's urine in detectable amounts very early in pregnancy, hence it is used in pregnancy test kits ✓

practice.q: Explain how oxytocin brings about birth through a positive feedback mechanism.
practice.memo:
• Stretching of the cervix late in pregnancy stimulates the release of oxytocin from the posterior pituitary ✓
• Oxytocin causes the myometrium to contract ✓
• Contractions push the baby further against the cervix, stretching it more ✓
• This stimulates even more oxytocin release, intensifying contractions until birth occurs (positive feedback) ✓

practice.q: List the THREE stages of labour in the correct order.
practice.memo:
• Dilation (effacement) of the cervix ✓
• Delivery/birth of the baby ✓
• Delivery of the placenta (afterbirth) ✓

practice.q: Explain how the contraceptive pill prevents pregnancy.
practice.memo:
• Contains synthetic oestrogen and progesterone ✓
• These maintain artificially high hormone levels, which negatively feedback on the hypothalamus/pituitary ✓
• FSH and LH secretion is suppressed ✓
• Without sufficient FSH/LH, follicle development and ovulation do not occur, so no egg is available for fertilisation ✓

practice.q: Compare a vasectomy and tubal ligation in terms of which structures are altered and the effect on hormone production.
practice.memo:
• Vasectomy: vas deferens is cut/sealed in the male; testosterone production in the testes is unaffected ✓✓
• Tubal ligation: fallopian tubes are cut/sealed in the female; ovarian hormone production (oestrogen/progesterone) and ovulation continue unaffected ✓✓

practice.q: Give TWO reasons why condoms are recommended even when another method of contraception (e.g. the pill) is being used.
practice.memo:
• Condoms protect against sexually transmitted infections (e.g. HIV), which hormonal methods do not ✓
• Using two methods together provides extra protection against unwanted pregnancy (dual protection) ✓

practice.q: Explain how HIV affects the human immune system, leading to AIDS.
practice.memo:
• HIV infects and destroys T-helper (CD4) lymphocytes ✓✓
• These cells normally coordinate the immune response against pathogens ✓
• As their numbers decline, the body becomes unable to fight off infections/cancers effectively ✓
• This advanced, severely immunocompromised stage is called AIDS ✓

practice.q: Discuss THREE ways in which the spread of HIV/AIDS can be reduced in a community.
practice.memo:
• Consistent and correct condom use during sexual intercourse ✓✓
• Voluntary HIV testing and counselling, with early access to ARV treatment ✓✓
• Avoiding the sharing of needles/sharp instruments; using sterile equipment ✓✓
• PMTCT programmes for pregnant women ✓✓
• Education/awareness campaigns and reducing stigma to encourage testing ✓✓ (any 3 points, 2 marks each)

practice.q: Explain why HIV cannot be transmitted through casual contact such as hugging or sharing utensils.
practice.memo:
• HIV requires direct exchange of specific bodily fluids (blood, semen, vaginal secretions, breast milk) to be transmitted ✓
• It is not present in saliva/sweat in sufficient quantity and does not survive well outside the body, so casual contact carries no risk ✓

practice.q: Describe the process of in-vitro fertilisation (IVF).
practice.memo:
• Hormone treatment is given to stimulate the ovaries to produce multiple mature eggs (superovulation) ✓
• Eggs are surgically retrieved from the ovaries ✓
• Eggs are fertilised with sperm in a laboratory dish ✓
• The resulting embryo(s) are cultured for a few days ✓
• One or more healthy embryos are then inserted into the uterus for implantation ✓

practice.q: Explain why a couple might choose IVF over natural conception.
practice.memo:
• Blocked or damaged fallopian tubes preventing natural fertilisation ✓
• Low sperm count/motility in the male partner ✓
• Other unexplained infertility issues, allowing fertilisation to be assisted directly in the laboratory ✓ (any 3 valid reasons)

practice.q: Explain the hormonal basis of menopause.
practice.memo:
• The number of functional follicles in the ovaries declines with age ✓
• Ovaries become less responsive to FSH/LH and produce less oestrogen and progesterone ✓
• Without sufficient hormone levels, ovulation and menstruation stop permanently ✓

mcq.q: A tropism is defined as ...
opts: A growth movement of a plant in response to a stimulus, where the direction of response is determined by the direction of the stimulus
Any random movement of a plant
A movement response in animals only
mcq.exp: Correct answer: A. Tropisms are directional growth responses — positive (towards stimulus) or negative (away from stimulus).

mcq.q: Phototropism is a growth response to ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Phototropism is the directional growth response of a plant to light.

mcq.q: The stem of a plant typically shows ... phototropism, while the root typically shows ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Stems grow towards light (positive phototropism) to maximise photosynthesis; roots are usually insensitive or grow away from light (negative).

mcq.q: Geotropism (gravitropism) is a growth response to ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Geotropism is the directional growth response of roots and shoots to gravity.

mcq.q: Roots show ... geotropism while shoots show ... geotropism.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: A. Roots grow towards gravity/downward (positive); shoots grow away from gravity/upward (negative).

mcq.q: Thigmotropism is a directional growth response to ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Thigmotropism allows climbing plants (e.g. tendrils) to coil around a support they touch.

mcq.q: Hydrotropism describes the growth response of roots towards ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Roots grow towards areas of higher moisture in the soil (positive hydrotropism).

mcq.q: Chemotropism is the growth response of a plant structure to ...
opts: Chemical stimuli, e.g. growth of the pollen tube towards chemicals released by the ovule
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The pollen tube grows down the style towards chemical attractants released by the ovule — a classic example of chemotropism.

mcq.q: Nastic movements differ from tropisms in that nastic movements ...
opts: Are growth responses whose direction depends on the direction of the stimulus
Are temporary, reversible movements whose direction is NOT determined by the direction of the stimulus
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Nastic responses (e.g. the Venus flytrap closing, or leaves folding at night) are rapid and non-directional relative to the stimulus.

mcq.q: Auxin (e.g. IAA) is a plant hormone produced mainly in the ...
opts: Apical meristem/tip of the shoot (and young leaves)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Auxin is synthesised in actively growing regions such as the shoot apex and moves down the stem.

mcq.q: In a shoot exposed to unilateral (one-sided) light, auxin ...
opts: Migrates to/accumulates on the shaded (darker) side of the shoot
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Auxin is laterally transported away from light towards the shaded side of the shoot.

mcq.q: A higher concentration of auxin on the shaded side of a stem causes that side to ...
opts: Grow more slowly than the lit side
Elongate/grow faster than the lit side, causing the stem to bend towards the light
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. In stems, auxin stimulates cell elongation; more auxin on the shaded side makes it grow faster, bending the tip towards the light (positive phototropism).

mcq.q: In ROOTS, unlike in stems, a high concentration of auxin ...
opts: Stimulates cell elongation in the same way as in stems
INHIBITS cell elongation, because root cells are far more sensitive to auxin
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Root cells are much more sensitive to auxin; high concentrations actually inhibit elongation, explaining why roots curve in the opposite direction to shoots.

mcq.q: In a root exposed to unilateral light, auxin accumulates on the shaded side. This causes the shaded side to ...
opts: Grow faster, bending the root towards the light
Grow more slowly (inhibited) than the lit side, causing the root to bend AWAY from the light
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Because roots are inhibited by high auxin, the shaded (high-auxin) side grows slower, curving the root away from light — negative phototropism.

mcq.q: In a horizontally placed stem, auxin accumulates on the ... side due to gravity, causing that side to grow faster and the stem to bend ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Auxin settles on the lower side under gravity; in the stem this stimulates faster growth on the lower side, bending the shoot upward.

mcq.q: In a horizontally placed root, auxin accumulates on the lower side. Because roots are inhibited by high auxin, this causes the root to bend ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The lower (high-auxin, inhibited) side grows slower than the upper side, so the root curves downward into the soil.

mcq.q: Which statement correctly summarises auxin's dual effect in geotropism?
opts: Auxin always stimulates growth in both roots and stems equally
Auxin stimulates cell elongation in stems but inhibits it in roots at the same concentration
Auxin has no role in geotropism
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. This differential sensitivity to the same auxin concentration explains the opposite bending responses of roots and stems.

mcq.q: The classic Charles Darwin/Boysen-Jensen-type experiments (decapitating coleoptile tips, inserting mica barriers, using agar blocks) provided evidence that ...
opts: Light is required for plant growth
The tip of a coleoptile produces a mobile chemical (auxin) that diffuses down and controls growth/bending
Roots and shoots respond identically to auxin
Gravity has no effect on plants
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. These classic experiments demonstrated that a diffusible substance produced in the tip is responsible for the bending response.

mcq.q: If the tip of a coleoptile is removed (decapitated), the coleoptile will ...
opts: Continue to bend towards light normally
Stop growing/bending, since the main source of auxin has been removed
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Without the auxin-producing tip, no further differential growth/bending occurs.

mcq.q: If an opaque (light-blocking) cap is placed over ONLY the tip of a coleoptile, while the rest of the shoot is exposed to unilateral light, the coleoptile will ...
opts: Bend towards the light as normal
NOT bend, because the tip (the light-perceiving, auxin-producing region) is shielded from detecting the light direction
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. This shows that the tip specifically perceives the light direction needed to trigger lateral auxin movement.

mcq.q: Gibberellins are plant hormones primarily responsible for ...
opts: Stem elongation, seed germination and breaking of dormancy
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Gibberellins promote stem elongation, mobilise food reserves during germination, and help break seed/bud dormancy.

mcq.q: Cytokinins are plant hormones that primarily promote ...
opts: Cell division and delay senescence (ageing) of leaves
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Cytokinins stimulate cell division (cytokinesis) and, working with auxin, regulate shoot and root development; they also delay ageing.

mcq.q: Abscisic acid (ABA) is often called a 'stress hormone' because it ...
opts: Induces stomatal closure during water stress and maintains seed/bud dormancy
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. ABA accumulates under drought stress, triggering guard cells to close stomata, reducing water loss; it also keeps seeds/buds dormant until conditions are favourable.

mcq.q: Ethylene (a gaseous hormone) is mainly responsible for ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Ethylene triggers the ripening process in fruit and the formation of an abscission layer leading to leaf/fruit drop.

mcq.q: The interaction between auxin and ethylene at the base of a leaf petiole, where decreasing auxin levels lead to increased ethylene production, results in ...
opts: Formation of an abscission layer, causing the leaf to fall off (leaf abscission)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. As auxin from an ageing leaf declines, ethylene increases, triggering enzymes that weaken the petiole base, leading to abscission.

mcq.q: The ratio of auxin to cytokinin applied to plant tissue in culture affects development; a HIGH auxin : cytokinin ratio tends to promote ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. In tissue culture, high auxin relative to cytokinin favours root development, while high cytokinin relative to auxin favours shoot development.

mcq.q: Photoperiodism refers to a plant's response to ...
opts: The relative length of day and night (photoperiod), e.g. controlling flowering time
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Photoperiodism governs processes such as flowering, which is triggered in many plants by a specific critical day/night length.

mcq.q: A short-day plant will flower when ...
opts: The night length is shorter than its critical night length
The (uninterrupted) night length is longer than (or equal to) its critical night length
Day length is continuous (24 hours of light)
It receives no light at all
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Short-day (= long-night) plants flower when the dark period exceeds a critical length; interrupting that dark period with a flash of light prevents flowering.

mcq.q: A long-day plant will flower when ...
opts: The night length exceeds its critical length
The night length is SHORTER than its critical (maximum) night length, i.e. day length is sufficiently long
It is kept in total darkness
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Long-day (= short-night) plants require nights shorter than a critical length in order to flower, typical of plants that flower in summer.

mcq.q: The pigment primarily responsible for detecting day length (red/far-red light) in photoperiodism is ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Phytochrome exists in two interconvertible forms (Pr and Pfr) and acts as the photoreceptor controlling flowering responses to day/night length.

mcq.q: In a controlled experiment testing phototropism, the use of a control (e.g. a seedling kept in the dark, or one lit from all sides) is important because it allows the researcher to ...
opts: Compare results and confirm that the response observed is due to the independent variable (light direction) being tested
Eliminate the need for a dependent variable
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. A control isolates the effect of the variable being investigated, ruling out other explanations for the observed growth pattern.

mcq.q: In a typical Grade 12 plant hormone investigation, the DEPENDENT variable when testing the effect of auxin concentration on root growth would be ...
opts: The length/amount of root growth measured
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The dependent variable is what is measured/observed as a result of changing the independent variable (auxin concentration).

practice.q: Define the term 'tropism' and distinguish between a positive and a negative tropism.
practice.memo:
• Tropism: a directional growth response of a plant to an external stimulus ✓
• Positive tropism: growth TOWARDS the stimulus ✓
• Negative tropism: growth AWAY from the stimulus ✓

practice.q: Name FOUR types of tropisms and the stimulus each responds to.
practice.memo:
• Phototropism — light ✓
• Geotropism — gravity ✓
• Thigmotropism — touch ✓
• Hydrotropism — water/moisture ✓ (any 4, e.g. chemotropism — chemicals also accepted)

practice.q: Explain the adaptive value (importance) of roots showing positive hydrotropism.
practice.memo:
• Roots grow towards areas of higher moisture in the soil ✓
• This increases the plant's chances of absorbing sufficient water for survival/physiological processes ✓

practice.q: Explain, in terms of auxin distribution, why a shoot bends towards a source of unilateral light.
practice.memo:
• Auxin is produced in the tip of the shoot ✓
• Under unilateral light, auxin is transported laterally and accumulates on the shaded side ✓
• Auxin stimulates cell elongation in the stem ✓
• Cells on the shaded side elongate more than cells on the lit side ✓
• This differential growth causes the shoot to bend towards the light (positive phototropism) ✓

practice.q: A scientist places a young root horizontally. Explain, in terms of auxin, why the root bends downward (into the soil).
practice.memo:
• Auxin accumulates on the lower side of the root due to gravity ✓
• Root cells are highly sensitive to auxin, so high auxin concentration INHIBITS cell elongation ✓
• The lower side therefore grows more slowly than the upper side ✓
• Differential growth (upper side longer than lower side) causes the root to curve downward ✓
• This is called positive geotropism ✓

practice.q: Describe a controlled investigation, using coleoptiles, agar blocks and mica, to show that auxin is a mobile chemical substance (not a physical effect of light itself).
practice.memo:
• Cut off the tips of several coleoptiles and place each tip on an agar block for a set time to allow auxin to diffuse into the agar ✓✓
• Place an auxin-containing agar block off-centre on top of a decapped coleoptile stump ✓
• Insert a mica (impermeable) barrier on one side between the agar block and the stump in some samples (control) ✓
• Observe that the coleoptile bends away from the side with the agar block (where auxin diffused down) when no light is involved ✓✓
• This shows the bending is due to a diffusible chemical (auxin), since light/gravity were excluded as variables ✓

practice.q: Compare the response of roots and stems to the SAME high concentration of auxin, and explain the physiological reason for the difference.
practice.memo:
• Stems: high auxin STIMULATES cell elongation/growth ✓
• Roots: the SAME concentration INHIBITS cell elongation/growth ✓
• This is because root cells are much more sensitive to auxin than stem cells ✓
• This explains why roots and shoots bend in opposite directions in geotropism (roots towards gravity, shoots away from it) ✓

practice.q: Tabulate (or list) FOUR plant hormones, stating ONE function of each.
practice.memo:
• Gibberellins — stem elongation/breaking seed dormancy ✓
• Cytokinins — promote cell division/delay senescence ✓
• Abscisic acid — stomatal closure during water stress/maintains dormancy ✓
• Ethylene — fruit ripening/leaf abscission ✓

practice.q: Explain how abscisic acid helps a plant survive periods of drought.
practice.memo:
• Water stress triggers increased ABA production ✓
• ABA causes guard cells to lose turgor, closing the stomata ✓
• This reduces transpiration/water loss from the plant, helping it conserve water ✓

practice.q: Explain the role of auxin and ethylene in leaf abscission (leaf fall).
practice.memo:
• As a leaf ages, auxin production in the leaf decreases ✓
• Falling auxin levels lead to an increase in ethylene at the base of the petiole ✓
• Ethylene stimulates the formation of an abscission layer, weakening the petiole until the leaf falls ✓

practice.q: A learner sets up an investigation to test the effect of unilateral light on the direction of growth of bean seedlings. State the investigation's aim, independent variable, dependent variable and a suitable control.
practice.memo:
• Aim: to investigate the effect of unilateral (one-sided) light on the direction of growth of bean seedlings ✓
• Independent variable: direction/presence of light (unilateral vs none/all-round) ✓
• Dependent variable: direction of growth/degree of bending of the seedling ✓
• Control: a seedling grown in the dark, or with light from all sides equally, for comparison ✓

practice.q: Explain why a chrysanthemum (a short-day plant) fails to flower if its dark period is interrupted by a brief flash of light each night.
practice.memo:
• Chrysanthemums require an uninterrupted night length longer than their critical night length to flower ✓
• A flash of light interrupts/resets the dark period, so the required uninterrupted night length is never reached ✓
• Without this signal, flowering is not triggered, even though total darkness hours may be sufficient ✓

practice.q: Explain why it is important to use a large sample size (many seedlings) and to repeat a plant hormone investigation.
practice.memo:
• A larger sample reduces the effect of individual variation between plants on the results ✓
• Repeating the investigation improves reliability and allows anomalies to be identified and an average to be calculated ✓

mcq.q: The endocrine system communicates using ...
opts: Hormones (chemical messengers) secreted into the blood by glands
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Endocrine glands are ductless and secrete hormones directly into the bloodstream to act on distant target organs.

mcq.q: Compared to nervous responses, hormonal responses are generally ...
opts: Slower to start but longer-lasting in effect
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Hormones travel via the blood (slower than nerve impulses) but tend to produce more prolonged effects.

mcq.q: The pituitary gland is often called the 'master gland' because it ...
opts: Secretes hormones that regulate the activity of many other endocrine glands
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The pituitary, especially its anterior lobe, releases tropic hormones (e.g. TSH, ACTH, FSH, LH) that control other glands.

mcq.q: The pituitary gland is controlled by the ...
opts: Hypothalamus, via releasing hormones and neural connections
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The hypothalamus links the nervous and endocrine systems, regulating pituitary hormone release.

mcq.q: Growth hormone (GH/somatotropin), secreted by the anterior pituitary, functions to ...
opts: Stimulate growth of bones and muscles, and protein synthesis
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. GH promotes growth in children/adolescents and regulates metabolism throughout life.

mcq.q: Hyposecretion of growth hormone during childhood results in ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Too little GH during the growing years leads to abnormally short stature.

mcq.q: Hypersecretion of growth hormone during childhood (before bone growth plates fuse) results in ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Excess GH before puberty causes excessive long bone growth, resulting in gigantism.

mcq.q: Hypersecretion of growth hormone in ADULTS (after growth plates have fused) results in ...
opts: Acromegaly (thickening of bones in hands, feet and face)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Since long bones can no longer lengthen, excess GH instead causes thickening of bones and soft tissue, especially in extremities.

mcq.q: The thyroid gland secretes thyroxine, which functions to ...
opts: Regulate the body's basal metabolic rate (BMR)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Thyroxine increases the metabolic rate of most body cells, affecting energy production, growth and development.

mcq.q: Thyroxine production requires sufficient dietary ... as a key raw material.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Iodine is an essential component of the thyroxine molecule; a deficiency leads to goitre.

mcq.q: A goitre (enlarged thyroid gland) is most commonly caused by ...
opts: Iodine deficiency, leading to reduced thyroxine production and continuous TSH stimulation
A tumour of the pituitary only
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Low iodine means low thyroxine; lack of negative feedback causes the pituitary to keep releasing TSH, over-stimulating and enlarging the thyroid.

mcq.q: Hyperthyroidism (overactive thyroid) typically causes symptoms such as ...
opts: Weight loss, rapid heart rate, nervousness/increased metabolism
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Excess thyroxine speeds up metabolism, producing symptoms like weight loss, increased heart rate and heat intolerance.

mcq.q: Hypothyroidism (underactive thyroid) typically causes symptoms such as ...
opts: Weight loss and rapid heart rate
Weight gain, fatigue, cold intolerance and slowed metabolism
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Insufficient thyroxine slows metabolic rate, leading to lethargy, weight gain and sensitivity to cold.

mcq.q: The parathyroid glands secrete parathyroid hormone (PTH), which functions to ...
opts: Raise blood calcium levels by stimulating its release from bone, increasing absorption in the gut/kidney
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. PTH raises blood Ca2+ levels when they fall too low, which is vital for nerve and muscle function.

mcq.q: The adrenal medulla secretes ... in response to stress (the 'fight or flight' response).
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Adrenaline increases heart rate, blood glucose and blood flow to muscles, preparing the body for rapid action.

mcq.q: The adrenal cortex secretes hormones including cortisol, which functions to ...
opts: Raise blood glucose (via gluconeogenesis) and help the body respond to long-term stress
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Cortisol mobilises energy reserves and suppresses non-essential functions during prolonged stress.

mcq.q: The pancreas, acting as an endocrine gland, secretes the hormones ...
opts: Insulin and glucagon, from the islets of Langerhans
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Beta cells secrete insulin (lowers blood glucose); alpha cells secrete glucagon (raises blood glucose).

mcq.q: Insulin lowers blood glucose levels by ...
opts: Stimulating the liver to break down glycogen into glucose
Stimulating cells (e.g. liver, muscle) to absorb glucose from the blood and convert it to glycogen for storage
Stimulating the kidneys to excrete water
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Insulin promotes glucose uptake by cells and glycogenesis (glucose → glycogen) in the liver and muscles.

mcq.q: Glucagon raises blood glucose levels by ...
opts: Stimulating the liver to break down glycogen into glucose (glycogenolysis) and release it into the blood
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Glucagon and insulin work antagonistically to keep blood glucose within a narrow range.

mcq.q: Type 1 diabetes mellitus results from ...
opts: The pancreas producing little or no insulin (often autoimmune destruction of beta cells)
Resistance of body cells to insulin only, with normal insulin production
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Type 1 diabetes is typically due to insufficient insulin production, often requiring insulin injections.

mcq.q: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is typically characterised by ...
opts: Insulin resistance, where body cells respond poorly to insulin, often associated with lifestyle factors
Excess glucagon production with normal insulin sensitivity
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. In Type 2 diabetes, the body still produces insulin (at least initially) but cells become less responsive to it.

mcq.q: The hormone melatonin, secreted by the pineal gland, primarily regulates ...
opts: Sleep-wake cycles (circadian rhythm) in response to light/dark
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Melatonin secretion increases in darkness, promoting sleepiness, and decreases in light.

mcq.q: Negative feedback in the endocrine system means that ...
opts: A hormone's effect amplifies its own further production
A change caused by a hormone reduces/switches off further secretion of that hormone, maintaining homeostasis
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Most endocrine control loops use negative feedback to keep hormone/related variable levels within a stable range.

mcq.q: Steroid hormones (e.g. oestrogen, testosterone, cortisol) typically act on target cells by ...
opts: Binding to receptors on the cell surface membrane only
Diffusing through the cell membrane (being lipid-soluble) and binding to receptors inside the cell, often affecting gene expression
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Steroid hormones are lipid-soluble and pass through the membrane to bind intracellular receptors, often altering transcription.

mcq.q: Protein/peptide hormones (e.g. insulin, growth hormone) typically act on target cells by ...
opts: Diffusing freely through the cell membrane
Binding to receptors on the cell surface membrane, triggering a second-messenger response inside the cell
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Being water-soluble, protein hormones cannot cross the membrane directly and instead bind surface receptors.

mcq.q: A target organ/cell is defined as one that ...
opts: Has specific receptors for a particular hormone and therefore responds to it
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Only cells bearing the correct receptor will respond to a given hormone, explaining hormone specificity.

mcq.q: The thymus gland, most active in childhood, secretes thymosin, which functions to ...
opts: Stimulate the development and maturation of T-lymphocytes (immune cells)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The thymus plays a key role in developing the immune system early in life.

mcq.q: Addison's disease results from ...
opts: Hypersecretion of cortisol by the adrenal cortex
Hyposecretion of cortisol (and aldosterone) by the adrenal cortex
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Addison's disease is caused by underactive adrenal cortex function, leading to fatigue, weight loss and low blood pressure.

mcq.q: Cushing's syndrome results from ...
opts: Hypersecretion of cortisol (or prolonged use of corticosteroid medication)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Excess cortisol causes symptoms such as weight gain (especially around the trunk/face), high blood pressure and muscle weakness.

mcq.q: In a typical negative feedback loop involving the hypothalamus, pituitary and a target gland (e.g. thyroid), rising levels of the target gland's hormone will ...
opts: Stimulate the hypothalamus/pituitary to release more tropic hormone
Inhibit the hypothalamus/pituitary, reducing release of the tropic hormone (e.g. TRH/TSH)
Have no effect on the pituitary
Permanently shut down the target gland
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. This negative feedback loop prevents over-production of the target hormone, maintaining homeostasis.

mcq.q: Which of the following hormone pairs act in an ANTAGONISTIC (opposing) manner?
mcq.exp: Correct answer: A. Insulin lowers and glucagon raises blood glucose — classic antagonistic hormone pair. (TSH stimulates thyroxine release rather than opposing it.)

mcq.q: A doctor measures abnormally HIGH TSH but LOW thyroxine in a patient's blood. The most likely explanation is ...
opts: A pituitary tumour secreting excess TSH with a normally functioning thyroid
A problem with the thyroid gland itself (it cannot respond adequately to TSH, e.g. due to iodine deficiency), so negative feedback fails to switch off TSH release
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. If the thyroid cannot produce enough thyroxine despite high TSH stimulation, thyroxine remains low and the pituitary keeps secreting more TSH since negative feedback never reduces it.

mcq.q: Diabetes insipidus (different from diabetes mellitus) is caused by a deficiency of ...
opts: Antidiuretic hormone (ADH), resulting in excessive urine production
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Diabetes insipidus results from insufficient ADH, so the kidneys cannot reabsorb enough water, causing excessive dilute urine.

mcq.q: Endocrine glands differ from exocrine glands in that endocrine glands ...
opts: Secrete their products through ducts onto a surface
Are ductless and secrete hormones directly into the bloodstream
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Exocrine glands (e.g. sweat, salivary glands) use ducts; endocrine glands release hormones straight into the blood.

practice.q: Distinguish between the nervous system and the endocrine system in terms of the type of message sent and the speed/duration of the response.
practice.memo:
• Nervous system: uses electrical impulses (and neurotransmitters at synapses); responses are fast and short-lived ✓✓
• Endocrine system: uses hormones transported in the blood; responses are slower to start but longer-lasting ✓✓

practice.q: Explain the difference between gigantism and acromegaly in terms of their cause and the age at which they occur.
practice.memo:
• Gigantism: caused by hypersecretion of growth hormone DURING childhood, before the bone growth plates fuse, resulting in excessive height ✓✓
• Acromegaly: caused by hypersecretion of growth hormone in ADULTS, after bone growth plates have fused; long bones cannot lengthen further so bones thicken (hands, feet, face) ✓✓

practice.q: Explain, using negative feedback, why a person with an iodine-deficient diet may develop a goitre.
practice.memo:
• Low iodine intake means the thyroid cannot produce sufficient thyroxine ✓
• Low thyroxine fails to provide enough negative feedback to the pituitary/hypothalamus ✓
• TSH continues to be released/levels rise ✓
• Continuous over-stimulation by TSH causes the thyroid gland to enlarge ✓
• This enlargement is seen as a goitre ✓

practice.q: Explain how the body lowers blood glucose levels after a carbohydrate-rich meal.
practice.memo:
• Rising blood glucose is detected by beta cells in the islets of Langerhans (pancreas) ✓
• Beta cells secrete insulin into the blood ✓
• Insulin stimulates body cells (e.g. liver, muscle) to absorb glucose from the blood ✓
• Insulin stimulates the liver to convert excess glucose into glycogen for storage (glycogenesis), lowering blood glucose back to normal ✓

practice.q: Compare Type 1 and Type 2 diabetes mellitus in terms of cause.
practice.memo:
• Type 1: the pancreas produces little/no insulin (often due to autoimmune destruction of beta cells); usually develops early in life ✓✓
• Type 2: the pancreas may still produce insulin, but body cells become resistant/less responsive to it; often linked to obesity/lifestyle and develops later in life ✓✓

practice.q: Describe THREE physiological effects of adrenaline that prepare the body for a 'fight or flight' response.
practice.memo:
• Increases heart rate and breathing rate ✓
• Increases blood glucose levels (energy availability) ✓
• Diverts blood flow to skeletal muscles (away from digestive system) ✓ (any 3)

practice.q: A patient presents with weight gain, constant tiredness and intolerance to cold. Identify the most likely hormonal disorder and explain the underlying cause.
practice.memo:
• Disorder: hypothyroidism ✓
• Cause: insufficient thyroxine secretion by the thyroid gland ✓
• Explanation: thyroxine controls metabolic rate; too little thyroxine slows metabolism, causing these symptoms ✓

practice.q: Distinguish between an endocrine gland and an exocrine gland, giving an example of each.
practice.memo:
• Endocrine gland: ductless; secretes hormones directly into the bloodstream, e.g. thyroid gland ✓✓
• Exocrine gland: has a duct; secretes products onto a surface/into a cavity, e.g. sweat gland or salivary gland ✓✓

practice.q: Explain why insulin (a protein hormone) cannot simply diffuse into its target cells, and describe how it brings about its effect instead.
practice.memo:
• Insulin is water-soluble/too large to pass through the lipid bilayer of the cell membrane ✓
• It binds to specific receptor proteins on the surface of target cell membranes ✓
• This receptor binding triggers a response inside the cell (e.g. movement of glucose transporters to the membrane) without insulin itself entering the cell ✓

practice.q: A patient has a tumour on the thyroid gland causing it to secrete excess thyroxine continuously, independent of TSH levels. Predict and explain the effect this would have on TSH levels.
practice.memo:
• TSH levels would be LOW/suppressed ✓
• The high thyroxine level provides strong negative feedback on the hypothalamus and pituitary ✓
• This inhibits the release of TRH and TSH, even though the thyroid itself keeps over-producing thyroxine independently ✓

practice.q: Compare Addison's disease and Cushing's syndrome in terms of cortisol levels and general effect on the body.
practice.memo:
• Addison's disease: LOW cortisol (adrenal cortex underactive); leads to fatigue, weight loss, low blood pressure ✓✓
• Cushing's syndrome: HIGH cortisol (adrenal cortex overactive or excess corticosteroid use); leads to weight gain, high blood pressure, muscle weakness ✓✓

mcq.q: Homeostasis is best defined as ...
opts: The continuous growth of an organism
The maintenance of a relatively stable/constant internal environment despite external changes
The breakdown of food for energy
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Homeostasis keeps internal conditions (temperature, water balance, glucose, pH, etc.) within narrow limits for cells to function optimally.

mcq.q: A negative feedback mechanism works by ...
opts: Amplifying a change away from the set point
Detecting a deviation from the set point and triggering a response that returns the variable back towards the set point
Having no influence on body systems
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Negative feedback is the principal mechanism maintaining homeostasis in the body.

mcq.q: A receptor in a homeostatic control system functions to ...
opts: Detect a change/stimulus in the internal or external environment
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Receptors (e.g. thermoreceptors, osmoreceptors) monitor specific variables and send information to a control centre.

mcq.q: An effector in a homeostatic control system functions to ...
opts: Carry out the response that corrects the deviation (e.g. a muscle or gland)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Effectors are typically muscles or glands that produce the actual physiological response.

mcq.q: The control centre for thermoregulation in the body is the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The hypothalamus contains thermoreceptors and coordinates responses to changes in core body temperature.

mcq.q: When body temperature rises above normal, sweat glands respond by ...
opts: Increasing sweat secretion; evaporation of sweat cools the skin surface
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Evaporative cooling from increased sweating helps lower body temperature.

mcq.q: Vasodilation of skin blood vessels (arterioles) when the body is too hot results in ...
opts: Less blood flow near the skin surface, reducing heat loss
More blood flow near the skin surface, increasing heat loss by radiation
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Widening of skin arterioles brings more warm blood close to the surface, where heat is radiated away.

mcq.q: Vasoconstriction of skin blood vessels when the body is too cold results in ...
opts: Increased heat loss from the skin
Reduced blood flow to the skin surface, conserving heat in the body core
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Narrowing skin arterioles diverts blood away from the surface, minimising heat loss to the environment.

mcq.q: Shivering helps to raise body temperature because ...
opts: Rapid, involuntary muscle contractions generate heat as a by-product of cellular respiration
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Shivering thermogenesis produces extra heat through increased muscular activity.

mcq.q: Erector pili muscles, which raise hair/'goosebumps' in response to cold, function in humans to ...
opts: Effectively trap a thick layer of insulating air (more significant in furred animals; in humans this response is largely vestigial)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: A. In hairy mammals, raised hairs trap an insulating air layer; in humans (less hair) the effect is much reduced but the reflex remains.

mcq.q: Behavioural thermoregulation (e.g. putting on a jacket, seeking shade) is an important mechanism because it ...
opts: Works alongside physiological responses to help maintain body temperature, especially when physiological responses alone are insufficient
Only occurs in animals, not humans
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Voluntary behaviour complements involuntary physiological mechanisms in thermoregulation.

mcq.q: Why is it important that body temperature is tightly regulated around 37 °C?
opts: Enzymes have an optimum temperature at which they function best; deviations reduce metabolic efficiency or denature enzymes
Temperature has no effect on enzymes
It only matters for muscle function
Cold temperatures always increase enzyme activity
mcq.exp: Correct answer: A. Most human enzymes work optimally around 37 °C; too high denatures them, too low slows reaction rates drastically.

mcq.q: Osmoregulation refers to the control of ...
opts: Water and solute (salt) balance in the body
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Osmoregulation maintains the correct concentration/osmotic balance of body fluids.

mcq.q: Osmoreceptors that detect changes in blood water concentration are located in the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The hypothalamus monitors blood osmotic concentration and triggers ADH release accordingly.

mcq.q: Antidiuretic hormone (ADH), released by the posterior pituitary, functions to ...
opts: Decrease water reabsorption in the kidney, producing more dilute urine
Increase the permeability of the kidney's collecting ducts to water, increasing water reabsorption and producing more concentrated urine
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. ADH makes the collecting duct walls more permeable to water, allowing more water to be reabsorbed back into the blood.

mcq.q: If a person is dehydrated (blood is too concentrated), the body responds by ...
opts: Increasing ADH secretion, leading to increased water reabsorption and concentrated (small volume) urine
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. More ADH causes the kidneys to conserve water, helping restore normal blood concentration.

mcq.q: If a person drinks a large volume of water (blood is too dilute), the body responds by ...
opts: Decreasing ADH secretion, leading to less water reabsorption and large volumes of dilute urine
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Less ADH means collecting ducts are less permeable to water, so excess water is excreted as dilute urine.

mcq.q: The functional unit of the kidney responsible for filtration and reabsorption is the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Each kidney contains roughly a million nephrons, each consisting of a glomerulus, Bowman's capsule and a series of tubules.

mcq.q: Ultrafiltration in the glomerulus/Bowman's capsule forces small molecules (water, glucose, salts, urea) out of the blood due to ...
opts: High hydrostatic (blood) pressure in the glomerulus capillaries
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The narrow efferent arteriole creates high pressure in the glomerulus, forcing fluid and small solutes into Bowman's capsule.

mcq.q: Glucose is normally ... reabsorbed from the filtrate back into the blood in a healthy person.
opts: Completely (100%), mainly in the proximal convoluted tubule by active transport/co-transport
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. All filtered glucose is normally reabsorbed; glucose in urine (glycosuria) indicates a problem such as diabetes mellitus.

mcq.q: The loop of Henle's main function is to ...
opts: Create a concentration (osmotic) gradient in the medulla, enabling the kidney to produce concentrated urine when needed
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The countercurrent multiplier mechanism in the loop of Henle establishes the salt gradient used by the collecting duct to reabsorb water.

mcq.q: Aldosterone, secreted by the adrenal cortex, functions to ...
opts: Increase sodium (Na+) reabsorption in the kidney tubules, which indirectly promotes water retention
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Aldosterone regulates electrolyte (especially Na+/K+) balance and blood volume/pressure.

mcq.q: Urea, a waste product excreted in urine, is formed in the ... from the breakdown of excess ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The liver deaminates excess amino acids, converting toxic ammonia into the less toxic urea, which is then excreted by the kidneys.

mcq.q: The renal artery carries blood ... the kidney, and the renal vein carries blood ... the kidney.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The renal artery supplies blood (with wastes) TO the kidney for filtration; the renal vein returns filtered blood FROM the kidney to circulation.

mcq.q: Urine passes from the kidney to the bladder via the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Each ureter carries urine from a kidney down to the bladder for temporary storage.

mcq.q: Urine is expelled from the bladder to outside the body via the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The urethra carries urine (and, in males, semen) out of the body.

mcq.q: The Bowman's capsule surrounds a network of capillaries called the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The glomerulus is the capillary knot where ultrafiltration of blood occurs.

mcq.q: Selective reabsorption in the proximal convoluted tubule involves the active transport of substances such as ...
opts: Glucose, amino acids and salts back into the blood
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Useful substances filtered out during ultrafiltration are reabsorbed back into the blood, mostly in the proximal tubule.

mcq.q: Dialysis is a medical treatment used for patients with kidney failure, which works by ...
opts: Replacing the kidneys with a healthy organ permanently in all cases
Filtering the patient's blood through a machine/membrane to remove waste products and excess water, mimicking kidney function
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Dialysis artificially performs the filtering role of the kidneys for patients whose kidneys cannot function adequately.

mcq.q: Which of the following is NOT normally found in the filtrate (in Bowman's capsule) of a healthy person?
opts: Large plasma proteins and blood cells
mcq.exp: Correct answer: D. Plasma proteins and blood cells are too large to pass through the capillary walls during ultrafiltration and remain in the blood.

mcq.q: A learner is given a graph showing blood ADH concentration rising sharply after exercising in hot weather without drinking water. The most likely explanation is that ...
opts: The blood became more dilute due to sweating
Sweating caused water loss, making the blood more concentrated, which stimulated osmoreceptors to trigger increased ADH release
ADH has no link to sweating
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Water loss through sweat raises blood solute concentration, detected by the hypothalamus, increasing ADH secretion to conserve water.

mcq.q: Excessive intake of alcohol is known to ... ADH secretion, which explains why alcohol consumption leads to increased, dilute urine production.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Alcohol suppresses ADH release, reducing water reabsorption and producing more dilute urine, contributing to dehydration.

practice.q: Describe the homeostatic mechanisms that occur when the body becomes too HOT.
practice.memo:
• Thermoreceptors in skin/hypothalamus detect the rise in temperature ✓
• Hypothalamus coordinates responses ✓
• Sweat glands increase sweat secretion; evaporation cools the skin ✓✓
• Skin arterioles vasodilate, increasing blood flow to skin surface so more heat is lost by radiation ✓✓
• Erector pili muscles relax, hair lies flat (reduces insulation) ✓

practice.q: Describe the homeostatic mechanisms that occur when the body becomes too COLD.
practice.memo:
• Thermoreceptors detect the drop in temperature ✓
• Hypothalamus coordinates responses ✓
• Skin arterioles vasoconstrict, reducing blood flow to the skin surface, conserving heat ✓✓
• Shivering: rapid muscle contractions generate heat ✓
• Erector pili muscles contract, raising hairs to trap an insulating air layer ✓
• Sweat gland activity decreases ✓ (any 6 marks worth)

practice.q: Explain why an athlete's body temperature can rise dangerously during a long-distance race in hot weather, and explain the risk of dehydration this poses.
practice.memo:
• Strenuous exercise produces large amounts of metabolic heat ✓
• Hot external conditions reduce the rate of heat loss by radiation ✓
