• The body compensates mainly through sweating, which leads to significant water and electrolyte loss ✓
• Excessive fluid loss without replacement can cause dehydration, reducing blood volume and impairing further cooling ✓

practice.q: Explain how the body responds, in terms of ADH secretion and kidney function, when a person is severely dehydrated.
practice.memo:
• Osmoreceptors in the hypothalamus detect that the blood is too concentrated (low water content) ✓
• This stimulates increased secretion of ADH from the posterior pituitary ✓
• ADH increases the permeability of the collecting ducts (and distal tubule) to water ✓
• More water is reabsorbed back into the blood ✓
• A small volume of concentrated urine is produced, conserving water ✓

practice.q: Describe the process of ultrafiltration in the nephron.
practice.memo:
• Occurs in the glomerulus and Bowman's capsule ✓
• High blood/hydrostatic pressure (due to the narrower efferent arteriole) forces fluid out of the glomerular capillaries ✓
• Water, glucose, salts, urea and other small molecules pass into the Bowman's capsule (forming filtrate) ✓
• Large molecules such as proteins and blood cells remain in the blood (too large to filter) ✓

practice.q: Explain why glucose is found in the urine of a person with untreated diabetes mellitus, even though glucose is normally completely reabsorbed.
practice.memo:
• In diabetes, blood glucose levels are abnormally high (hyperglycaemia) ✓
• The amount of glucose filtered into the nephron exceeds the tubule's maximum reabsorption capacity (transport maximum) ✓
• Excess, unreabsorbed glucose remains in the filtrate and is excreted in the urine (glycosuria) ✓

practice.q: Distinguish between the roles of ADH and aldosterone in regulating the body's water and salt balance.
practice.memo:
• ADH: regulates WATER reabsorption directly, by altering permeability of the collecting duct ✓✓
• Aldosterone: regulates SODIUM (Na+) reabsorption; water follows osmotically, indirectly affecting blood volume ✓✓

practice.q: Name the structures, in order, through which filtrate/urine passes from the glomerulus to outside the body.
practice.memo: • Glomerulus/Bowman's capsule → Proximal convoluted tubule → Loop of Henle → Distal convoluted tubule → Collecting duct → Ureter → Bladder → Urethra ✓✓✓✓✓ (1 mark per 2 correctly ordered structures, max 5)

practice.q: Explain why a person who drinks excessive amounts of alcohol often needs to urinate frequently and may become dehydrated.
practice.memo:
• Alcohol inhibits the secretion of ADH from the posterior pituitary ✓
• Lower ADH reduces the permeability of the collecting duct to water ✓
• Less water is reabsorbed, so larger volumes of dilute urine are produced ✓
• This excessive water loss can lead to dehydration ✓

practice.q: Using the concept of negative feedback, explain in general terms how the body returns an internal variable (e.g. temperature or blood glucose) to its normal range after a deviation.
practice.memo:
• A receptor detects the deviation from the set point/normal range ✓
• Information is sent to a control centre (e.g. hypothalamus or pancreas), which coordinates a response ✓
• An effector (muscle or gland) brings about a corrective action that opposes/reverses the deviation ✓
• Once the variable returns to normal, the original stimulus is removed/reduced, switching off the corrective response (negative feedback) ✓

mcq.q: External fertilisation, typical of most fish and amphibians, occurs ...
opts: Outside the body, usually in water, where eggs and sperm are released and meet
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. External fertilisation requires a watery environment to prevent gametes from drying out and to allow sperm to swim to the eggs.

mcq.q: Internal fertilisation, typical of reptiles, birds and mammals, occurs ...
opts: Inside the female's body, after the male deposits sperm directly into the female reproductive tract
Only after the egg has been laid
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Internal fertilisation does not depend on water and gives gametes greater protection.

mcq.q: Fish typically produce a very LARGE number of eggs because ...
opts: Fertilisation success and survival of external eggs/young is generally low, so high numbers compensate for high mortality
mcq.exp: Correct answer: A. External fertilisation and lack of parental care in most fish mean very few offspring survive, so high egg numbers increase the chance some will survive.

mcq.q: Amphibians typically lay their eggs ...
opts: In water or moist environments, since their eggs lack a shell and would dry out otherwise
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Amphibian eggs have a jelly-like (not waterproof) covering, so they require a moist/aquatic environment to prevent desiccation.

mcq.q: The amniotic egg, found in reptiles and birds, is a key adaptation because it ...
opts: Must be laid in water like amphibian eggs
Contains a fluid-filled amniotic sac and other membranes, allowing the embryo to develop on dry land without drying out
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The amniotic egg, with its shell and membranes (amnion, chorion, allantois, yolk sac), was a key evolutionary adaptation enabling vertebrates to reproduce away from water.

mcq.q: In the amniotic egg, the function of the allantois is to ...
opts: Store metabolic waste (e.g. uric acid) and assist in gas exchange
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The allantois acts as a waste-storage sac and works with the chorion in gas exchange across the shell.

mcq.q: In the amniotic egg, the function of the yolk sac is to ...
opts: Provide a food (nutrient) reserve for the developing embryo
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The yolk provides the nutrients needed for embryonic development inside the shell.

mcq.q: In the amniotic egg, the amnion functions to ...
opts: Surround the embryo in fluid, cushioning it against shock and preventing it from drying out
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Amniotic fluid protects the embryo, similar to the role of amniotic fluid in mammalian pregnancy.

mcq.q: Birds and reptiles typically lay FEWER eggs than fish mainly because ...
opts: Internal fertilisation and the protective amniotic egg/parental care greatly increase the chances of survival of each offspring
They cannot produce many eggs physically
mcq.exp: Correct answer: A. Greater protection (shell, internal fertilisation, parental care/incubation) means fewer offspring are needed to ensure species survival.

mcq.q: Viviparity (giving birth to live young, typical of most mammals) differs from oviparity (egg-laying) in that ...
opts: The embryo develops and is nourished inside the mother's body via a placenta, rather than inside a shelled egg
Viviparous animals always lay eggs first
mcq.exp: Correct answer: A. In viviparous mammals, the embryo develops in the uterus, receiving nourishment directly from the mother via the placenta.

mcq.q: Which of the following represents a GENERAL trend in vertebrate reproductive strategy from fish to mammals?
opts: Decreasing parental care and decreasing egg/offspring protection
Increasing parental care, increasing protection of offspring (e.g. amniotic egg, viviparity), and a corresponding decrease in the number of offspring produced
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. As reproductive strategies evolved greater protection/care, fewer offspring were needed to maintain populations, trading quantity for quality of care.

mcq.q: Monotremes (e.g. the platypus) are unusual mammals because they ...
opts: Give birth to live young like other mammals
Lay eggs, despite still being mammals that produce milk for their young
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Monotremes are an evolutionarily distinct group of egg-laying mammals.

mcq.q: Marsupials (e.g. kangaroos) are characterised by ...
opts: A fully developed placenta supporting long gestation
Giving birth to relatively undeveloped young that complete development while attached to a teat, often in a pouch
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Marsupial young are born very early in development and continue growing externally, nursing in the mother's pouch.

mcq.q: Placental mammals (eutherians) are characterised by ...
opts: A short gestation period with undeveloped young at birth
A well-developed placenta that allows extended gestation, with young born at a more advanced stage of development
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The placenta allows for prolonged, efficient nutrient/gas exchange, supporting longer development inside the mother before birth.

mcq.q: A disadvantage of external fertilisation compared to internal fertilisation is that ...
opts: It requires no water at all
Gametes are exposed to the environment and may be lost/diluted, washed away, or eaten by predators before fertilisation occurs
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Because sperm and eggs are released into open water, many gametes never meet, lowering fertilisation success.

mcq.q: Amphibian larvae (e.g. tadpoles) typically ...
opts: Are identical in form to the adult
Undergo metamorphosis, changing from an aquatic, gill-breathing larval form into a more terrestrial, lung-breathing adult form
Develop inside a shelled egg on land
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Metamorphosis is a key feature of amphibian development, reflecting their dual aquatic/terrestrial lifestyle.

mcq.q: Bird eggs require incubation (being kept warm, usually by a parent sitting on them) because ...
opts: Embryonic development requires a fairly constant, warm temperature for enzymes to function optimally
Eggs need to be cooled to hatch
It has no effect on development
mcq.exp: Correct answer: A. Stable warmth supports normal enzyme activity and metabolic processes needed for the embryo to develop properly.

mcq.q: Which of the following animal groups shows internal fertilisation but typically lays an unshelled, jelly-coated egg in water?
opts: Most amphibians (though fertilisation is usually external in most species)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Most amphibians actually use external fertilisation in water with a jelly-coated egg; this distinguishes them from the shelled, internally-fertilised amniotic eggs of reptiles/birds.

mcq.q: Parental care in vertebrates generally ... as you move from fish to mammals.
opts: Increases, correlating with a decrease in the number of offspring produced
Has no relationship with offspring number
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Greater investment in fewer offspring (K-selection-like strategy) is typical of mammals and birds compared to fish.

mcq.q: Gestation period refers to ...
opts: The time taken for an egg to be laid
The length of time a developing embryo/foetus is carried inside the mother's uterus before birth (in viviparous animals)
The incubation period of bird eggs only
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Gestation period varies greatly between mammal species, generally correlating with body size and developmental maturity at birth.

practice.q: Compare external and internal fertilisation, naming an example of an animal group that uses each and explaining ONE advantage of internal fertilisation.
practice.memo:
• External fertilisation: gametes released into the environment (usually water), e.g. fish/amphibians ✓
• Internal fertilisation: sperm deposited directly into the female's body, e.g. reptiles/birds/mammals ✓
• Advantage of internal fertilisation: gametes are protected from drying out/predation, and is not dependent on water, increasing fertilisation success ✓✓

practice.q: Name THREE extra-embryonic membranes/structures found in an amniotic egg and state the function of each.
practice.memo:
• Amnion — surrounds the embryo in fluid, cushioning it and preventing desiccation ✓✓
• Chorion — surrounds all other membranes, involved in gas exchange ✓✓
• Allantois — stores nitrogenous waste and assists in gas exchange ✓✓
• Yolk sac — provides a food/nutrient reserve ✓✓ (any 3 for full marks)

practice.q: Explain why fish generally produce thousands of eggs, while most birds produce only a few eggs per breeding season.
practice.memo:
• Fish: external fertilisation in water with no parental care of eggs/young means a very low survival rate, so many eggs are needed to ensure some offspring survive ✓✓
• Birds: internal fertilisation, a protective shelled (amniotic) egg, incubation and significant parental care greatly increase survival chances of each individual offspring, so fewer eggs are needed ✓✓

practice.q: Distinguish between monotremes, marsupials and placental mammals in terms of how their young develop.
practice.memo:
• Monotremes: lay eggs; young hatch and are then nourished with milk, e.g. platypus ✓✓
• Marsupials: give birth to poorly-developed young, which continue developing while attached to a teat (often in a pouch), e.g. kangaroo ✓✓
• Placental mammals: develop fully inside the uterus, nourished via a placenta, and are born at a relatively advanced stage, e.g. humans ✓✓

practice.q: Tabulate TWO differences between oviparous (egg-laying) and viviparous (live-bearing) reproduction.
practice.memo:
• Oviparous: embryo develops OUTSIDE the mother's body, inside an egg, nourished by yolk; e.g. birds/reptiles ✓✓
• Viviparous: embryo develops INSIDE the mother's body (uterus), nourished via a placenta; e.g. most mammals ✓✓

practice.q: Explain why most amphibians must return to water to reproduce, even though many live on land as adults.
practice.memo:
• Amphibian eggs lack a shell/waterproof covering and would dry out (desiccate) on land ✓
• Fertilisation is usually external, requiring water for sperm to swim to the eggs ✓
• The aquatic larval stage (e.g. tadpole) also needs water to develop, with gills for breathing ✓

practice.q: Explain TWO reasons why incubation of eggs by a parent bird increases the chances of successful hatching.
practice.memo:
• Maintains a constant, optimal temperature needed for normal embryonic enzyme activity/development ✓✓
• Protects the eggs from predators and physical damage while the parent guards the nest ✓✓

mcq.q: The function of dendrites on a neuron is to ...
opts: Transmit impulses away from the cell body
Receive impulses/signals and conduct them towards the cell body
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Dendrites receive input from other neurons or receptors and carry the signal toward the cell body.

mcq.q: The axon of a neuron functions to ...
opts: Conduct (transmit) the nerve impulse away from the cell body towards the synapse/effector
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The axon carries impulses away from the cell body, often over long distances.

mcq.q: The myelin sheath, formed by Schwann cells, functions to ...
opts: Insulate the axon and increase the speed of impulse transmission via saltatory conduction
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Myelin insulates the axon; impulses 'jump' between the gaps (nodes of Ranvier), speeding up transmission.

mcq.q: The gaps in the myelin sheath are called ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. At the nodes of Ranvier, the axon membrane is exposed, allowing rapid saltatory conduction of the impulse.

mcq.q: A sensory (afferent) neuron functions to ...
opts: Carry impulses from the CNS to an effector
Carry impulses from a receptor towards the central nervous system (CNS)
Connect two neurons in the brain only
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Sensory neurons transmit information about stimuli detected by receptors to the CNS.

mcq.q: A motor (efferent) neuron functions to ...
opts: Carry impulses from a receptor to the CNS
Carry impulses from the CNS to an effector (muscle or gland)
Connect sensory and motor neurons within the CNS only
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Motor neurons carry the 'output' signal from the CNS to bring about a response.

mcq.q: An interneuron (relay/connector neuron), found within the CNS, functions to ...
opts: Connect sensory and motor neurons, integrating and relaying information within the brain/spinal cord
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Interneurons process and relay information between sensory input and motor output within the CNS.

mcq.q: A synapse is best described as ...
opts: A direct physical/electrical connection between two neurons with no gap
A small gap between two neurons (or a neuron and effector) across which a nerve impulse is transmitted chemically
The myelinated part of an axon
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. At a synapse, electrical impulses are converted to a chemical signal (neurotransmitter) that crosses the synaptic cleft.

mcq.q: At a synapse, the arrival of a nerve impulse causes the ...
opts: Presynaptic neuron's vesicles to release neurotransmitter into the synaptic cleft
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Neurotransmitter, stored in vesicles in the presynaptic terminal, is released by exocytosis when an impulse arrives.

mcq.q: Neurotransmitter released at a synapse diffuses across the synaptic cleft and binds to receptors on the ...
opts: Postsynaptic membrane, triggering a new impulse in the next neuron (if threshold is reached)
Cell body of the same neuron
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Binding of neurotransmitter to receptors on the postsynaptic membrane can trigger depolarisation and a new impulse.

mcq.q: Synapses ensure that nerve impulses travel in ONE direction only because ...
opts: Neurotransmitter is only released from the presynaptic side, and only the postsynaptic membrane has the appropriate receptors
Impulses can travel either way equally
The synaptic cleft blocks all signals
mcq.exp: Correct answer: A. This one-way chemical relay mechanism ensures impulses move in a single direction through a neural pathway.

mcq.q: Neurotransmitter remaining in the synaptic cleft after transmission is typically ...
opts: Broken down by enzymes or reabsorbed, preventing continuous stimulation of the postsynaptic neuron
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Removal of neurotransmitter (enzymatic breakdown or reuptake) is essential to reset the synapse for the next impulse.

mcq.q: A resting (unstimulated) neuron's membrane is described as ...
opts: Polarised, with the inside more negative than the outside (resting potential)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. At rest, sodium-potassium pumps and selective permeability maintain a negative resting potential inside the axon.

mcq.q: During depolarisation of an axon membrane (the nerve impulse), ...
opts: Potassium ions rush out, making the inside more negative
Sodium ion channels open and Na+ rushes into the axon, making the inside temporarily positive relative to the outside
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The rapid influx of Na+ reverses the polarity of the membrane at that point, propagating the impulse along the axon.

mcq.q: The central nervous system (CNS) consists of the ...
opts: Sensory and motor neurons outside the CNS
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The brain and spinal cord together form the CNS, the body's main control and processing centre.

mcq.q: The peripheral nervous system (PNS) consists of ...
opts: All nerves (sensory and motor) outside the brain and spinal cord, connecting the CNS to the rest of the body
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The PNS includes cranial and spinal nerves that link the CNS to receptors and effectors throughout the body.

mcq.q: The cerebrum, the largest part of the human brain, is mainly responsible for ...
opts: Higher mental functions such as conscious thought, memory, reasoning, and voluntary movement
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The cerebrum (cerebral cortex) controls conscious processes, including sensory interpretation, voluntary movement and intellect.

mcq.q: The cerebellum is mainly responsible for ...
opts: Coordinating balance, posture and fine muscular movement
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The cerebellum fine-tunes motor activity, ensuring smooth, coordinated movement and balance.

mcq.q: The medulla oblongata, part of the brainstem, controls ...
opts: Vital involuntary functions such as heart rate, breathing rate and blood pressure
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The medulla oblongata regulates essential autonomic functions necessary for survival.

mcq.q: The autonomic nervous system controls ...
opts: Involuntary functions of internal organs (e.g. heart rate, digestion), divided into sympathetic and parasympathetic divisions
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The autonomic nervous system regulates internal processes automatically, without conscious control.

mcq.q: The sympathetic nervous system generally prepares the body for ...
opts: 'Fight or flight' — increasing heart rate, diverting blood to muscles, etc.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The sympathetic division becomes dominant during stress/danger, mobilising the body for rapid action.

mcq.q: The parasympathetic nervous system generally promotes ...
opts: 'Rest and digest' functions — slowing heart rate, stimulating digestion, conserving energy
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The parasympathetic division dominates during calm conditions, restoring and conserving bodily resources.

mcq.q: A reflex arc is best described as ...
opts: A consciously planned response to a stimulus
A rapid, involuntary, automatic response to a stimulus, bypassing conscious brain processing for speed
A response that only occurs in plants
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Reflexes protect the body by allowing very fast responses (e.g. withdrawing a hand from heat) without waiting for conscious decision-making.

mcq.q: The correct general pathway of a reflex arc is ...
opts: Effector → receptor → sensory neuron → motor neuron
Receptor → sensory neuron → relay/interneuron (in spinal cord) → motor neuron → effector
Receptor → motor neuron → effector → sensory neuron
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. This is the standard pathway: stimulus detected by receptor, signal passed via sensory neuron to spinal cord, relayed to motor neuron, which stimulates the effector.

mcq.q: Reflex actions are advantageous because they ...
opts: Occur very rapidly, protecting the body from harm before the brain consciously processes the stimulus
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Bypassing the brain (using the spinal cord as the main relay point) saves crucial time in dangerous situations.

mcq.q: The transparent front layer of the eye that begins to refract (bend) light entering the eye is the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The cornea is the main light-refracting structure, focusing light before it reaches the lens.

mcq.q: The function of the iris is to ...
opts: Focus light precisely on the retina
Control the amount of light entering the eye by adjusting the size of the pupil
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The iris contains muscles that constrict or dilate the pupil depending on light intensity.

mcq.q: In BRIGHT light, the pupil ... due to contraction of the ... muscles of the iris.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Circular muscles of the iris contract in bright light, narrowing the pupil to limit light entry and protect the retina.

mcq.q: In DIM light, the pupil ... due to contraction of the ... muscles of the iris.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Radial muscles of the iris contract in dim light, widening the pupil to allow more light in.

mcq.q: The lens of the eye functions to ...
opts: Detect light and convert it into nerve impulses
Fine-focus light onto the retina by changing shape (accommodation)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The lens adjusts its shape to focus on objects at different distances — a process called accommodation.

mcq.q: During accommodation for viewing a NEAR object, the ciliary muscles ... and the lens becomes ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Contraction of the ciliary muscles reduces tension on the suspensory ligaments, allowing the elastic lens to become more rounded for greater refraction needed to focus near objects.

mcq.q: During accommodation for viewing a DISTANT object, the ciliary muscles ... and the lens becomes ...
opts: Relax; thin/flattened, increasing tension on the suspensory ligaments
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Relaxed ciliary muscles increase tension on the suspensory ligaments, flattening the lens for less refraction needed for distant objects.

mcq.q: The retina contains photoreceptor cells called rods and cones. Rods are mainly responsible for ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Rods are highly sensitive to low light intensity but cannot distinguish colour.

mcq.q: Cones, found in the retina, are mainly responsible for ...
opts: Colour vision and visual acuity (sharpness) in bright light
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Cones require brighter light and allow detailed, colour vision, concentrated especially in the fovea.

mcq.q: The blind spot on the retina is the point where ...
opts: The fovea is located, with the highest concentration of cones
The optic nerve leaves the eye, with no photoreceptors present
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. There are no rods or cones where the optic nerve exits the eye, creating a small blind spot in the visual field.

mcq.q: Myopia (short-sightedness) occurs when ...
opts: The eyeball is too short, or the lens too weak, focusing the image behind the retina
The eyeball is too long, or the lens refracts too strongly, focusing the image in front of the retina, making distant objects appear blurry
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Myopia is corrected using a concave (diverging) lens to move the focal point back onto the retina.

mcq.q: Hyperopia (long-sightedness) occurs when ...
opts: The eyeball is too short, or the lens too weak, focusing the image behind the retina, making near objects appear blurry
The image always focuses correctly on the retina
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Hyperopia is corrected using a convex (converging) lens to bring the focal point forward onto the retina.

mcq.q: Sound waves entering the ear first cause vibration of the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Sound waves funnelled by the outer ear/pinna cause the eardrum to vibrate.

mcq.q: Vibrations from the eardrum are transmitted and amplified by the three small bones (ossicles) of the middle ear, namely the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. These three tiny bones mechanically amplify and transmit vibrations from the eardrum to the oval window of the cochlea.

mcq.q: The Eustachian tube connects the middle ear to the ...
opts: Throat (pharynx), equalising air pressure on either side of the eardrum
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. This pressure-equalising function explains the 'popping' sensation felt when ascending/descending in an aeroplane.

mcq.q: The cochlea, in the inner ear, contains receptor cells responsible for ...
opts: Converting sound vibrations into nerve impulses (hearing)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Hair cells within the fluid-filled cochlea convert mechanical vibrations into electrical nerve impulses sent to the brain via the auditory nerve.

mcq.q: The semicircular canals of the inner ear are responsible for detecting ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Fluid movement within the three semicircular canals (oriented in different planes) provides information about head rotation, contributing to balance.

mcq.q: The utricle and saccule, located in the vestibule of the inner ear, detect ...
opts: Linear acceleration and the position of the head relative to gravity (static balance)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. These structures contain receptors sensitive to gravity and linear movement, important for maintaining posture and balance.

practice.q: Describe the structure of a neuron, naming FOUR parts and stating the function of each.
practice.memo:
• Dendrites — receive impulses and carry them towards the cell body ✓✓
• Cell body (contains the nucleus) — controls the neuron's metabolic activities ✓✓
• Axon — conducts the impulse away from the cell body ✓✓
• Myelin sheath — insulates the axon and speeds up impulse transmission ✓✓ (any 4 structures with correct function, 2 marks each)

practice.q: Describe how a nerve impulse is transmitted across a synapse.
practice.memo:
• The nerve impulse arrives at the presynaptic terminal/knob ✓
• This triggers synaptic vesicles to move to and fuse with the presynaptic membrane, releasing neurotransmitter by exocytosis ✓✓
• Neurotransmitter diffuses across the synaptic cleft ✓
• It binds to specific receptors on the postsynaptic membrane ✓
• This triggers depolarisation/a new impulse in the postsynaptic neuron (if threshold reached) ✓

practice.q: Explain why synapses ensure one-directional flow of nerve impulses, and explain the importance of neurotransmitter being broken down/removed after use.
practice.memo:
• Neurotransmitter is released only from the presynaptic side and receptors are only on the postsynaptic membrane, so impulses can only cross in one direction ✓✓
• Breakdown/removal of neurotransmitter prevents continuous/repeated stimulation of the postsynaptic neuron, allowing the synapse to reset for the next impulse ✓✓

practice.q: Distinguish between the central nervous system (CNS) and the peripheral nervous system (PNS), naming the structures that make up each.
practice.memo:
• CNS: brain and spinal cord; main centre for processing/coordinating information ✓✓
• PNS: all nerves (cranial and spinal) outside the CNS; connects the CNS to receptors and effectors throughout the body ✓✓

practice.q: Describe, using the correct sequence of structures, what happens when a person accidentally touches a hot object and rapidly withdraws their hand.
practice.memo:
• A receptor (e.g. in the skin) detects the painful/heat stimulus ✓
• A sensory neuron carries the impulse to the spinal cord (CNS) ✓
• A relay/interneuron in the spinal cord passes the impulse to a motor neuron ✓
• The motor neuron carries the impulse to an effector (a muscle in the arm) ✓
• The muscle contracts, withdrawing the hand ✓
• This occurs rapidly and involuntarily, without first requiring conscious processing by the brain ✓

practice.q: Name the part of the brain responsible for EACH of the following: (a) conscious thought, (b) balance and coordination, (c) regulating heart rate and breathing.
practice.memo:
(a) Cerebrum ✓
(b) Cerebellum ✓
(c) Medulla oblongata ✓

practice.q: Compare the sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems in terms of their general effect on the body.
practice.memo:
• Sympathetic: prepares the body for action/stress ('fight or flight'); increases heart rate, redirects blood to muscles, dilates pupils ✓✓
• Parasympathetic: promotes relaxation and restoration ('rest and digest'); decreases heart rate, stimulates digestion ✓✓

practice.q: Describe the pupillary reflex that occurs when a person moves from a dimly lit room into bright sunlight.
practice.memo:
• Bright light is detected by receptors (and via the optic nerve) ✓
• Circular muscles of the iris contract (radial muscles relax) ✓
• The pupil constricts/becomes smaller ✓
• This reduces the amount of light entering the eye, protecting the retina ✓

practice.q: Explain the process of accommodation that occurs when a person shifts their focus from a distant object to a near object.
practice.memo:
• Ciliary muscles contract ✓
• Tension on the suspensory ligaments decreases ✓
• The elastic lens becomes thicker/more rounded (more convex) ✓
• This increases the degree of refraction of light ✓
• The image of the near object is focused correctly on the retina ✓

practice.q: Distinguish between myopia and hyperopia in terms of cause and method of correction.
practice.memo:
• Myopia (short-sightedness): image focuses in FRONT of the retina (eyeball too long/lens too strong); corrected with a CONCAVE (diverging) lens ✓✓
• Hyperopia (long-sightedness): image focuses BEHIND the retina (eyeball too short/lens too weak); corrected with a CONVEX (converging) lens ✓✓

practice.q: Compare rods and cones in terms of their sensitivity to light and the type of vision they provide.
practice.memo:
• Rods: very sensitive to low light intensity; provide black-and-white/dim-light vision; cannot detect colour ✓✓
• Cones: require brighter light; provide colour vision and high visual acuity, concentrated at the fovea ✓✓

practice.q: Describe the pathway of a sound wave from the outer ear to the point where it is converted into a nerve impulse.
practice.memo:
• Sound waves are funnelled by the pinna into the ear canal ✓
• They cause the tympanic membrane (eardrum) to vibrate ✓
• Vibrations are transmitted and amplified by the hammer, anvil and stirrup (ossicles) ✓
• The stirrup transmits vibrations to the oval window of the cochlea ✓
• Fluid movement in the cochlea stimulates hair cells, converting vibrations into nerve impulses, sent to the brain via the auditory nerve ✓

practice.q: Explain the function of the semicircular canals and the utricle/saccule in maintaining balance.
practice.memo:
• Semicircular canals detect rotational movement of the head (e.g. spinning), via fluid movement stimulating hair cells ✓✓
• Utricle and saccule detect linear acceleration and the head's position relative to gravity (static balance) ✓✓

mcq.q: DNA is described as a double helix, meaning it consists of ...
opts: Two polynucleotide strands twisted around each other
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. DNA's double helix structure was described by Watson and Crick, based partly on Rosalind Franklin's X-ray data.

mcq.q: A nucleotide, the basic building block of DNA, consists of ...
opts: A phosphate group, a deoxyribose sugar, and a nitrogenous base
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. These three components join to form a nucleotide, which then links to others to form a polynucleotide strand.

mcq.q: The sugar found in DNA is ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Deoxyribose lacks one oxygen atom compared to ribose (found in RNA), giving DNA its name.

mcq.q: The four nitrogenous bases found in DNA are ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. DNA bases are A, T, G and C; RNA replaces Thymine with Uracil.

mcq.q: In DNA base pairing, Adenine always pairs with ..., and Guanine always pairs with ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. This is known as complementary base pairing: A–T and G–C, governed by hydrogen bonding (A-T = 2 H-bonds, G-C = 3 H-bonds).

mcq.q: Adenine and Guanine are classified as ... bases, while Thymine and Cytosine are classified as ... bases.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. A purine always pairs with a pyrimidine, keeping the width of the DNA helix constant.

mcq.q: The two strands of a DNA molecule are held together by ...
opts: Hydrogen bonds between complementary base pairs
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Relatively weak hydrogen bonds allow the strands to be 'unzipped' during replication and transcription.

mcq.q: The sugar-phosphate backbone of a DNA strand is held together by ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Phosphodiester bonds link the sugar of one nucleotide to the phosphate of the next, forming a strong, stable backbone.

mcq.q: The two strands of the DNA double helix run ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. One strand runs 5' to 3', while the complementary strand runs 3' to 5' — this antiparallel arrangement is essential for replication.

mcq.q: DNA is found mainly in the ... of a eukaryotic cell, packaged together with proteins called ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. DNA wraps around histone proteins to form chromatin, which condenses further into chromosomes during cell division.

mcq.q: DNA replication is described as semi-conservative because ...
opts: Both new DNA molecules are made entirely of new material
Each new DNA molecule consists of one original (parent) strand and one newly synthesised strand
Only one new DNA molecule is produced overall
DNA is destroyed during the process
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. This was confirmed by the Meselson-Stahl experiment; each daughter molecule retains half of the original DNA.

mcq.q: The enzyme primarily responsible for unwinding/unzipping the DNA double helix during replication is ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Helicase breaks the hydrogen bonds between base pairs, separating the two strands to expose the template.

mcq.q: The enzyme responsible for adding new complementary nucleotides to a template strand during DNA replication is ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. DNA polymerase reads the template strand and joins free nucleotides according to complementary base pairing rules.

mcq.q: DNA ligase functions during replication to ...
opts: Join Okazaki fragments together on the lagging strand, sealing gaps in the new strand
Add nucleotides continuously on the leading strand
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Ligase seals the nicks between fragments, particularly important on the discontinuously synthesised lagging strand.

mcq.q: Free nucleotides used during DNA replication are obtained from ...
opts: The pool of nucleotides available in the nucleoplasm/cell
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Free nucleotides present in the cell are used to build the new complementary strands.

mcq.q: The purpose of DNA replication is to ...
opts: Make an identical copy of the DNA, ensuring each daughter cell receives a complete, accurate set of genetic information during cell division
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Accurate replication before mitosis/meiosis ensures genetic continuity between generations of cells.

mcq.q: DNA replication occurs during which phase of the cell cycle?
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. DNA is duplicated during the S phase, before the cell divides in mitosis/meiosis.

mcq.q: Transcription is the process by which ...
opts: DNA is copied into another DNA molecule
A complementary mRNA copy is made from a DNA template (gene)
mRNA is translated into a protein
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Transcription occurs in the nucleus, producing mRNA which carries the genetic code to the ribosome.

mcq.q: The enzyme responsible for synthesising mRNA during transcription is ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. RNA polymerase reads the DNA template strand and assembles a complementary mRNA strand.

mcq.q: During transcription, mRNA is synthesised using one strand of DNA as a template; where DNA has the base Adenine, mRNA will have ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. RNA uses Uracil instead of Thymine; Uracil pairs with Adenine (and DNA Thymine pairs with mRNA Adenine).

mcq.q: After transcription in eukaryotic cells, the mRNA molecule typically undergoes processing, including ...
opts: Removal of non-coding sections (introns) and joining of coding sections (exons), known as splicing
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. RNA splicing removes introns, leaving only exons in the mature mRNA before it leaves the nucleus.

mcq.q: After transcription, the mature mRNA molecule moves from the nucleus to the ...
opts: Cytoplasm, where it attaches to a ribosome for translation
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. mRNA exits the nucleus through nuclear pores to reach ribosomes for the next stage, translation.

mcq.q: Translation is the process by which ...
opts: The genetic code carried by mRNA is used to assemble a chain of amino acids (a polypeptide/protein) at the ribosome
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Translation converts the nucleotide sequence (codons) on mRNA into a specific sequence of amino acids.

mcq.q: A codon is defined as ...
opts: A sequence of three nucleotide bases on mRNA that codes for a specific amino acid (or a start/stop signal)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The genetic code is read in triplets (codons); 64 possible codons code for 20 amino acids plus start/stop signals.

mcq.q: During translation, transfer RNA (tRNA) functions to ...
opts: Carry the genetic code from DNA to the ribosome
Carry a specific amino acid to the ribosome and pair its anticodon with the complementary codon on mRNA
Form the structure of the ribosome
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Each tRNA molecule has an anticodon that base-pairs with a specific mRNA codon, ensuring the correct amino acid is added.

mcq.q: An anticodon is found on ... and is complementary to a ... on mRNA.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The tRNA anticodon base-pairs with the mRNA codon, linking the correct amino acid into the growing polypeptide chain.

mcq.q: Ribosomes are the site of ...
opts: Translation, where amino acids are joined by peptide bonds to form a polypeptide
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Ribosomes (made of rRNA and protein) read mRNA codons and catalyse peptide bond formation between amino acids.

mcq.q: A 'stop' codon on mRNA signals that ...
opts: A new amino acid chain should begin
Translation should end and the completed polypeptide chain should be released from the ribosome
The mRNA should be transcribed again
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Stop codons (e.g. UAA, UAG, UGA) do not code for an amino acid; they terminate translation.

mcq.q: A mutation that changes a single base in DNA could potentially alter the corresponding ... and, in turn, the ... produced.
opts: Codon on mRNA; amino acid sequence of the protein
Number of ribosomes; rate of mitosis
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Since DNA codes for mRNA codons which specify amino acids, a DNA base change can alter the protein's amino acid sequence and function.

practice.q: Describe the structure of a single DNA nucleotide and explain how nucleotides join to form a polynucleotide strand.
practice.memo:
• A nucleotide consists of a phosphate group, a deoxyribose sugar, and a nitrogenous base ✓✓
• Nucleotides join via covalent (phosphodiester) bonds between the phosphate of one nucleotide and the sugar of the next, forming the sugar-phosphate backbone ✓✓

practice.q: Explain the rule of complementary base pairing and state why this rule is important during DNA replication.
practice.memo:
• Adenine always pairs with Thymine (A–T) and Guanine always pairs with Cytosine (G–C) ✓✓
• This is because purines (A, G) always pair with pyrimidines (T, C), keeping a constant helix width, and specific hydrogen bonding only occurs between these pairs ✓
• This rule ensures that each new DNA strand made during replication is an exact copy of the original (semi-conservative replication), maintaining genetic accuracy ✓

practice.q: Distinguish between the bonds holding the two strands of DNA together and the bonds within a single strand (sugar-phosphate backbone).
practice.memo:
• Between the two strands (between bases): weak hydrogen bonds, allowing the strands to separate/unzip during replication and transcription ✓✓
• Within a single strand (sugar to phosphate): strong covalent (phosphodiester) bonds, providing structural stability ✓✓

practice.q: Describe the process of DNA replication.
practice.memo:
• Helicase unwinds and separates the two strands of the DNA double helix by breaking hydrogen bonds ✓✓
• Each original (parent) strand acts as a template ✓
• DNA polymerase joins free nucleotides to each template strand, following complementary base pairing (A-T, G-C) ✓✓
• Ligase joins any fragments together, sealing the new strand ✓
• Two new DNA molecules are formed, each with one original and one new strand (semi-conservative) ✓

practice.q: Explain why DNA replication is essential before a cell undergoes mitosis.
practice.memo:
• Mitosis produces two daughter cells, each requiring a complete, identical set of chromosomes/DNA ✓
• Replication ensures the DNA amount is doubled BEFORE division ✓
• This allows each daughter cell to receive an identical, full copy of the genetic information after division ✓

practice.q: Describe the process of transcription.
practice.memo:
• RNA polymerase binds to and unwinds a section of DNA (the gene) ✓
• One strand of DNA acts as a template ✓
• RNA polymerase joins free RNA nucleotides complementary to the template strand (using Uracil instead of Thymine) ✓✓
• This forms a single-stranded mRNA molecule ✓
• The mRNA detaches and (after processing/splicing) moves out of the nucleus to the cytoplasm ✓

practice.q: Describe the process of translation, including the role of tRNA and ribosomes.
practice.memo:
• mRNA attaches to a ribosome in the cytoplasm ✓
• tRNA molecules, each carrying a specific amino acid, bring amino acids to the ribosome ✓
• The tRNA anticodon pairs with the complementary codon on the mRNA ✓✓
• The ribosome catalyses the formation of a peptide bond between adjacent amino acids ✓
• This continues, building a polypeptide chain, until a stop codon is reached and the chain is released ✓

practice.q: Using the central dogma (DNA → RNA → protein), explain how a change (mutation) in a single DNA base could result in a non-functional protein.
practice.memo:
• A change in a DNA base may alter the corresponding codon on the mRNA produced during transcription ✓
• This could code for a different amino acid (or a premature stop codon) during translation ✓
• A different amino acid sequence may change the protein's 3D shape/structure ✓
• If the shape changes significantly (e.g. at an active site), the protein may not function correctly ✓

practice.q: Distinguish between a codon and an anticodon.
practice.memo:
• Codon: a triplet of bases on mRNA that codes for a specific amino acid ✓
• Anticodon: a complementary triplet of bases on tRNA that pairs with the mRNA codon ✓

mcq.q: Meiosis is a type of cell division that produces ...
opts: Two diploid daughter cells genetically identical to the parent
Four haploid daughter cells, each genetically different from the parent and each other
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Meiosis halves the chromosome number (diploid → haploid) and introduces genetic variation, producing gametes.

mcq.q: The main biological significance of meiosis is to ...
opts: Allow growth and repair of body tissue
Produce haploid gametes and introduce genetic variation, so that fertilisation restores the diploid number and offspring differ genetically from parents
Produce identical copies of somatic cells
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Without meiosis halving the chromosome number, the chromosome number would double every generation at fertilisation.

mcq.q: Meiosis consists of ...
opts: One round of division only, like mitosis
Two successive rounds of division (Meiosis I and Meiosis II), but only ONE round of DNA replication
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. DNA replicates once before Meiosis I; Meiosis I and II then occur in succession without further replication.

mcq.q: During Prophase I of meiosis, homologous chromosomes ...
opts: Pair up (synapsis), forming bivalents/tetrads, and may exchange genetic material (crossing over)
Are replicated for the first time
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Homologous chromosome pairing and crossing over during Prophase I are key sources of genetic variation.

mcq.q: Crossing over refers to the exchange of ...
opts: Genetic material (segments) between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes at points called chiasmata
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Crossing over recombines alleles on homologous chromosomes, creating new combinations not present in either parent chromosome.

mcq.q: During Metaphase I of meiosis, homologous pairs (bivalents) align ...
opts: Singly along the equator, as in mitosis
In pairs along the equator (metaphase plate), with random orientation of each pair
At the poles of the cell
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Random (independent) orientation of each homologous pair contributes to genetic variation.

mcq.q: Independent assortment refers to ...
opts: The exchange of segments between chromatids
The random orientation/alignment of homologous chromosome pairs during Metaphase I, leading to random distribution of maternal and paternal chromosomes into gametes
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Independent assortment, together with crossing over, is a major source of genetic variation produced by meiosis.

mcq.q: During Anaphase I of meiosis, ... separate and move to opposite poles.
opts: Homologous chromosomes (each still consisting of two chromatids)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Unlike mitosis, it is whole homologous chromosomes (not sister chromatids) that separate during Anaphase I.

mcq.q: At the end of Meiosis I, the two daughter cells produced are ...
opts: Diploid, genetically identical to each other
Haploid, but each chromosome still consists of two sister chromatids
Diploid with four chromatids per chromosome
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Meiosis I reduces the chromosome number from diploid to haploid; Meiosis II then separates the sister chromatids.

mcq.q: Meiosis II is similar to mitosis in that, during Anaphase II, ... separate and move to opposite poles.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. As in mitosis, sister chromatids are pulled apart during Anaphase II, but starting from a haploid cell, resulting in 4 haploid cells overall.

mcq.q: At the end of meiosis (both divisions), a total of ... haploid daughter cells are produced from one original diploid cell.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. One diploid cell → 2 haploid cells (Meiosis I) → 4 haploid cells (Meiosis II).

mcq.q: Which of the following is a key DIFFERENCE between mitosis and meiosis?
opts: Mitosis produces 4 daughter cells; meiosis produces 2
Mitosis produces genetically identical diploid cells; meiosis produces genetically varied haploid cells
Mitosis involves homologous chromosome pairing; meiosis does not
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Mitosis is for growth/repair and maintains the chromosome number; meiosis halves it and creates variation for sexual reproduction.

mcq.q: Non-disjunction refers to ...
opts: The failure of homologous chromosomes (or sister chromatids) to separate properly during meiosis, resulting in gametes with an abnormal chromosome number
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Non-disjunction can lead to conditions such as Down syndrome (trisomy 21), where an extra chromosome 21 is present.

mcq.q: Down syndrome typically results from non-disjunction leading to an individual having ...
opts: Three copies of chromosome 21 (trisomy 21) instead of the normal two
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Trisomy 21 occurs when chromosome 21 fails to separate properly during meiosis in one of the parents' gametes.

mcq.q: Genetic variation produced by meiosis arises mainly from TWO processes, namely ...
opts: Crossing over (during Prophase I) and independent assortment (during Metaphase I)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. These two meiotic processes, together with random fertilisation, are the main sources of genetic variation in sexually reproducing organisms.

mcq.q: A bivalent (tetrad) formed during Prophase I consists of ...
opts: Two homologous chromosomes (four chromatids in total) closely paired together
Two sister chromatids of one chromosome only
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. A bivalent is formed when two homologous chromosomes (one maternal, one paternal), each with two sister chromatids, pair up during synapsis.

mcq.q: A chiasma (plural: chiasmata) is the point at which ...
opts: Non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes cross over and exchange genetic material
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Chiasmata are the visible X-shaped points where crossing over has occurred between homologous chromatids.

mcq.q: During Telophase I and cytokinesis of meiosis, the result is ...
opts: Two haploid cells, each chromosome still made of two sister chromatids
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Meiosis I produces two haploid cells; each division product still has chromosomes consisting of two chromatids until Meiosis II separates them.

mcq.q: Spermatogenesis and oogenesis both rely on the process of meiosis to ...
opts: Reduce the chromosome number by half, producing haploid gametes (sperm/egg cells)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Both gametogenesis processes use meiosis to ensure gametes are haploid, ready to restore the diploid number at fertilisation.

mcq.q: If a human diploid cell with 46 chromosomes undergoes meiosis, each resulting daughter cell will have ... chromosomes.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Meiosis halves the chromosome number; human gametes are haploid with 23 chromosomes.

mcq.q: Random/independent assortment combined with crossing over means that, theoretically, the number of genetically different gametes a human can produce is ...
opts: Always exactly the same, identical gametes
Extremely large/virtually unlimited, due to the many possible combinations of chromosomes and crossover points
Zero, since meiosis is identical to mitosis
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. With 23 pairs of chromosomes and random crossing over, the potential genetic variation among gametes is enormous.

practice.q: Explain why meiosis is necessary for sexual reproduction, in terms of chromosome number.
practice.memo:
• Meiosis reduces the chromosome number from diploid (2n) to haploid (n) in the gametes ✓✓
• If gametes were diploid, fertilisation would double the chromosome number every generation ✓
• Meiosis ensures that when two haploid gametes fuse at fertilisation, the normal diploid number is restored in the offspring ✓

practice.q: Explain how crossing over and independent assortment during meiosis contribute to genetic variation.
practice.memo:
• Crossing over: occurs during Prophase I; non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes exchange segments of genetic material at chiasmata ✓✓
• This creates NEW combinations of alleles on a chromosome that were not present in either parent chromosome ✓
• Independent assortment: during Metaphase I, homologous pairs align randomly at the equator ✓✓
• This results in random distribution of maternal and paternal chromosomes into daughter cells, creating many possible chromosome combinations in gametes ✓

practice.q: Compare mitosis and meiosis in terms of: (a) number of divisions, (b) number of daughter cells produced, (c) genetic composition of daughter cells relative to the parent cell.
practice.memo:
(a) Mitosis: ONE division; Meiosis: TWO divisions ✓✓
(b) Mitosis: 2 daughter cells; Meiosis: 4 daughter cells ✓✓
(c) Mitosis: daughter cells diploid and genetically IDENTICAL to parent; Meiosis: daughter cells haploid and genetically DIFFERENT from parent and each other ✓✓

practice.q: Explain, in terms of meiosis, how a baby could be born with Down syndrome (trisomy 21).
practice.memo:
• During meiosis (in the egg or sperm-forming parent), non-disjunction occurs ✓
• Homologous chromosome 21 fails to separate properly during Anaphase I (or sister chromatids fail to separate in Anaphase II) ✓
• This results in a gamete with TWO copies of chromosome 21 instead of one ✓
• When this gamete fuses with a normal gamete at fertilisation, the resulting zygote has three copies of chromosome 21 (trisomy 21/Down syndrome) ✓

practice.q: Briefly describe what happens during each of the following stages of Meiosis I: Prophase I, Metaphase I, Anaphase I.
practice.memo:
• Prophase I: homologous chromosomes pair up (synapsis) forming bivalents; crossing over may occur between non-sister chromatids ✓✓
• Metaphase I: bivalents (homologous pairs) align randomly along the equator/metaphase plate ✓✓
• Anaphase I: homologous chromosomes (each still with 2 chromatids) separate and move to opposite poles ✓✓

practice.q: Explain how meiosis and fertilisation together maintain a constant chromosome number across generations in a sexually reproducing species.
practice.memo:
• Meiosis halves the diploid chromosome number, producing haploid gametes ✓✓
• At fertilisation, a haploid sperm fuses with a haploid egg ✓
• This restores the diploid chromosome number in the resulting zygote, the same as in the parents ✓

mcq.q: A gene is best defined as ...
opts: A section of DNA that codes for a specific characteristic/protein
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Genes are units of heredity located at specific positions (loci) on chromosomes.

mcq.q: An allele is best described as ...
opts: A different version/form of a gene, occupying the same locus on homologous chromosomes
The physical appearance of an organism
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Alleles are alternative forms of a gene (e.g. for flower colour: a 'purple' allele and a 'white' allele).

mcq.q: Genotype refers to ...
opts: The physical/observable appearance of an organism
The genetic makeup (allele combination) of an organism for a particular trait
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Genotype is written using letters (e.g. Tt) representing the alleles an individual possesses.

mcq.q: Phenotype refers to ...
opts: The genetic makeup of an organism
The observable physical or biochemical characteristics of an organism, resulting from genotype and environment
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Phenotype is what you can actually observe/measure, e.g. tall or short, blood type, eye colour.

mcq.q: A homozygous genotype consists of ...
opts: Two different alleles for a gene (e.g. Tt)
Two identical alleles for a gene (e.g. TT or tt)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Homozygous individuals are 'true-breeding' for that trait, e.g. TT (homozygous dominant) or tt (homozygous recessive).

mcq.q: A heterozygous genotype consists of ...
opts: Two different alleles for a gene (e.g. Tt)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. A heterozygous individual carries one dominant and one recessive allele for that gene.

mcq.q: A dominant allele is one that ...
opts: Is only expressed when present in two copies
Is expressed in the phenotype even when only one copy is present (masking the recessive allele)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Dominant alleles are typically represented with a capital letter and mask the effect of a recessive allele in heterozygotes.

mcq.q: A recessive allele is one that ...
opts: Is always expressed in the phenotype
Is only expressed in the phenotype when present in the homozygous state (two copies, no dominant allele present)
Is dominant over all other alleles
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Recessive alleles are masked by a dominant allele in heterozygotes and only show in the phenotype when homozygous recessive.

mcq.q: In a monohybrid cross between two heterozygous individuals (Tt × Tt) for a trait with complete dominance, the expected phenotypic ratio in the offspring is ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Tt × Tt → 1 TT : 2 Tt : 1 tt genotypically, giving 3 dominant phenotype : 1 recessive phenotype.

mcq.q: In a monohybrid cross between a homozygous dominant individual and a homozygous recessive individual (TT × tt), the expected genotype of ALL offspring (F1) is ...
opts: A mixture of TT, Tt and tt
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. TT × tt can only produce offspring that are heterozygous (Tt) for that gene.

mcq.q: A test cross is used to determine ...
opts: The phenotype of an organism showing the dominant trait
Whether an organism showing the dominant phenotype is homozygous dominant or heterozygous, by crossing it with a homozygous recessive individual
The exact age of an organism
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. If ANY offspring show the recessive phenotype, the test organism must have been heterozygous; if all offspring show the dominant phenotype, it was likely homozygous dominant.

mcq.q: If a test cross (Tt × tt, or possibly TT × tt) produces offspring with a 1:1 ratio of dominant to recessive phenotypes, the original unknown parent was most likely ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. A 1:1 ratio in a test cross indicates the unknown parent was heterozygous, since Tt × tt → 1 Tt : 1 tt.

mcq.q: In pea plants, if Tall (T) is dominant over short (t), a cross between Tt and tt would be expected to produce offspring in a phenotypic ratio of ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Tt × tt → 1 Tt (tall) : 1 tt (short), giving a 1:1 ratio.

mcq.q: Codominance occurs when ...
opts: One allele completely masks the other
BOTH alleles in a heterozygote are fully and separately expressed in the phenotype (neither masks the other)
A new, blended phenotype is formed
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. In codominance, both alleles contribute their own distinct, visible effect, e.g. roan cattle coat colour (red AND white hairs both visible) or human blood group AB.

mcq.q: Incomplete dominance occurs when ...
opts: Both alleles are fully expressed separately
Heterozygotes show an intermediate (blended) phenotype between the two homozygous phenotypes
The recessive allele is never expressed
Only one allele exists for the gene
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. E.g. crossing red and white snapdragons produces pink offspring — a true blend, not a mix of red and white patches.

mcq.q: In cattle coat colour (an example of codominance), crossing a homozygous red bull (CRCR) with a homozygous white cow (CWCW) produces offspring that are ...
opts: Roan (CRCW) — showing both red AND white hairs distinctly, not blended
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Roan cattle display patches/intermixed red and white hairs because both alleles are expressed independently — true codominance, distinct from a blended pink.

mcq.q: In snapdragons (incomplete dominance for flower colour), crossing a red-flowered plant (CRCR) with a white-flowered plant (CWCW) produces F1 offspring that are ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Incomplete dominance produces a single blended intermediate phenotype in all heterozygotes.

mcq.q: If two pink snapdragons (CRCW, incomplete dominance) are crossed, the expected phenotypic ratio of the offspring is ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. CRCW × CRCW → 1 CRCR (red) : 2 CRCW (pink) : 1 CWCW (white).

mcq.q: The ABO human blood group system is an example of ... involving multiple alleles.
opts: Simple dominance only, with two alleles
Multiple alleles combined with codominance (IA and IB are codominant; both are dominant over i)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Three alleles exist (IA, IB, i); IA and IB are codominant with each other, and both are dominant over i.

mcq.q: A person with blood type AB has the genotype ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Type AB results from inheriting one IA and one IB allele, both of which are expressed on red blood cells.

mcq.q: A person with blood type O must have the genotype ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. Type O results only from two recessive 'i' alleles, since i is recessive to both IA and IB.

mcq.q: If a man with blood type AB (IAIB) has children with a woman who is type O (ii), the possible blood types of their children are ...
opts: Only type A (IAi) or type B (IBi)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. IAIB × ii → IAi (type A) and IBi (type B) offspring; type AB and type O are not possible from this particular cross.

mcq.q: Sex-linked inheritance refers to genes located on the ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Genes on the X (or rarely Y) chromosome show inheritance patterns linked to an individual's sex.

mcq.q: In humans, a male has the sex chromosome genotype ... and a female has ...
opts: XX; XY only in some cases
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Males are XY (one X from mother, one Y from father); females are XX (one X from each parent).

mcq.q: Red-green colour blindness is a classic example of an X-linked ... condition, more commonly seen in ...
opts: Recessive; males, because males only need one copy of the recessive allele (they have only one X chromosome) to show the condition
Recessive; equally common in males and females always
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Since males are XY, a single recessive allele on their only X chromosome will be expressed (they have no second X to carry a dominant allele).

mcq.q: Haemophilia is inherited as an X-linked recessive condition. A woman who is a carrier (heterozygous, XHXh) and a man who is unaffected (XHY) have children. What proportion of their SONS are expected to have haemophilia?
opts: 25% (1 in 4 of all children, i.e. half of sons)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. XHXh × XHY → daughters: XHXH, XHXh (unaffected, but possibly carriers); sons: XHY (unaffected), XhY (affected) — so half of sons (25% of total offspring) are affected.

mcq.q: A colour-blind father (XbY) and a homozygous normal-vision mother (XBXB) have children. Their daughters will be ...
opts: All carriers (XBXb) with normal vision, since they inherit XB from mother and Xb from father
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Daughters receive one X from each parent; XB (normal, dominant) from mother masks Xb (colour-blind) from father, making all daughters unaffected carriers.

mcq.q: Why can a female only show an X-linked recessive condition (like haemophilia) if she is homozygous recessive (XhXh)?
opts: Females do not have X chromosomes
Because she has two X chromosomes; a single dominant allele (XH) on either X chromosome would mask the recessive allele
Females cannot inherit X-linked traits at all
The Y chromosome carries the dominant allele
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. With two X chromosomes, a female needs two copies of the recessive allele to express the recessive phenotype, unlike males who need only one.

mcq.q: In a pedigree diagram, a horizontal line connecting two individuals typically represents ...
opts: A mating/marriage relationship between two individuals
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Horizontal lines link partners, while vertical/branching lines lead down to their offspring.

mcq.q: In a pedigree, a shaded (filled-in) symbol usually represents an individual who ...
opts: Is affected by/shows the condition/trait being studied
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Filled symbols indicate individuals expressing the phenotype of interest (e.g. the disorder).

mcq.q: If a genetic disorder appears to 'skip a generation' in a pedigree (unaffected parents have an affected child), this strongly suggests the condition is inherited as ...
opts: Recessive (both parents are likely unaffected carriers, i.e. heterozygous)
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. For a recessive condition, two unaffected heterozygous (carrier) parents can have an affected (homozygous recessive) child.

mcq.q: If EVERY affected individual in a pedigree has at least one affected parent, and the condition does not skip generations, this suggests the allele is most likely ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Dominant conditions typically appear in every generation, since only one copy of the dominant allele is needed for expression.

mcq.q: Cystic fibrosis is an example of a genetic disorder inherited in an ... pattern.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Cystic fibrosis requires two copies of the recessive allele (on an autosome, not a sex chromosome) to be expressed.

mcq.q: Huntington's disease is unusual among genetic disorders because it is inherited as an ... condition, meaning only ONE copy of the allele is needed to cause the disease.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Huntington's disease is a dominant disorder, so even heterozygous individuals are affected.

mcq.q: If both parents are unaffected carriers (heterozygous, Aa) for an autosomal recessive disorder, the probability that any given child will be affected (aa) is ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Aa × Aa → 1 AA : 2 Aa : 1 aa, giving a 1 in 4 (25%) chance of an affected (aa) child.

mcq.q: If one parent is affected (homozygous recessive, aa) and the other parent is an unaffected carrier (Aa) for an autosomal recessive disorder, the probability that a child will be affected is ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: C. aa × Aa → 1 Aa (unaffected carrier) : 1 aa (affected), giving a 50% chance of an affected child.

practice.q: Distinguish between the terms genotype and phenotype, using an example.
practice.memo:
• Genotype: the genetic makeup/allele combination of an organism, e.g. Tt ✓✓
• Phenotype: the observable physical characteristic resulting from the genotype, e.g. tall plant ✓✓

practice.q: Distinguish between homozygous and heterozygous genotypes, giving an example of each.
practice.memo:
• Homozygous: two identical alleles for a gene, e.g. TT or tt ✓✓
• Heterozygous: two different alleles for a gene, e.g. Tt ✓✓

practice.q: In guinea pigs, black fur (B) is dominant over white fur (b). A heterozygous black guinea pig is crossed with a white guinea pig. Using a genetic diagram (Punnett square), determine the expected genotype and phenotype ratios of the offspring.
practice.memo:
• Parental genotypes: Bb × bb ✓
• Gametes: B, b (from Bb) and b, b (from bb) ✓
• Punnett square: Bb, Bb, bb, bb ✓✓
• Genotype ratio: 1 Bb : 1 bb ✓
• Phenotype ratio: 1 black : 1 white (1:1) ✓

practice.q: Two pea plants, both showing the dominant tall phenotype, are crossed and produce offspring in a ratio of approximately 3 tall : 1 short. Determine the genotypes of the two parent plants and explain your reasoning.
practice.memo:
• Both parents must be heterozygous: Tt × Tt ✓✓
• Reasoning: since some offspring show the recessive (short) phenotype, both parents must carry a recessive allele, but since both show the dominant phenotype, both must also carry a dominant allele — therefore Tt × Tt ✓✓

practice.q: Explain the purpose of a test cross and describe how you would perform one to determine if a black guinea pig (black is dominant) is homozygous or heterozygous.
practice.memo:
• Purpose: to determine the unknown genotype of an organism showing the dominant phenotype ✓
• Method: cross the black guinea pig with a homozygous recessive (white) guinea pig ✓
• If ALL offspring are black, the original guinea pig is likely homozygous dominant (BB) ✓
• If approximately HALF the offspring are white, the original guinea pig is heterozygous (Bb) ✓

practice.q: Distinguish between codominance and incomplete dominance, using ONE example of each.
practice.memo:
• Codominance: both alleles are fully and separately expressed in the heterozygote, e.g. roan cattle coat colour (distinct red and white hairs both visible) ✓✓
• Incomplete dominance: the heterozygote shows a blended, intermediate phenotype, e.g. pink snapdragons from red × white parents ✓✓

practice.q: A woman with blood type A (genotype IAi) has children with a man with blood type B (genotype IBi). Using a genetic diagram, determine all the possible blood types (phenotypes) of their children.
practice.memo:
• Parental genotypes: IAi × IBi ✓
• Gametes: IA, i (mother) and IB, i (father) ✓
• Punnett square offspring genotypes: IAIB, IAi, IBi, ii ✓✓
• Possible phenotypes: AB, A, B, O ✓✓ (all four blood types are possible)

practice.q: A roan bull (CRCW) is crossed with a red cow (CRCR). Using a genetic diagram, determine the expected phenotype ratio of the offspring's coat colour.
practice.memo:
• Parental genotypes: CRCW × CRCR ✓
• Gametes: CR, CW (bull) and CR, CR (cow) ✓
• Punnett square: CRCR, CRCR, CRCW, CRCW ✓✓
• Phenotype ratio: 1 red : 1 roan (1:1); no white offspring possible from this cross ✓

practice.q: Explain why X-linked recessive conditions such as haemophilia are far more common in males than in females.
practice.memo:
• Males are XY, so they have only ONE X chromosome ✓
• If that single X carries the recessive allele, there is no second X (dominant allele) to mask it, so the condition is expressed ✓
• Females are XX and need TWO copies of the recessive allele (homozygous recessive) to express the condition, which is statistically less likely ✓
• A female with only one recessive allele (heterozygous) is an unaffected carrier ✓

practice.q: A carrier woman for haemophilia (XHXh) has children with a man with normal blood clotting (XHY). Using a genetic diagram, determine the genotypes and phenotypes of the possible offspring.
practice.memo:
• Parental genotypes: XHXh × XHY ✓
• Gametes: XH, Xh (mother) and XH, Y (father) ✓
• Offspring genotypes: XHXH (normal female), XHXh (carrier female), XHY (normal male), XhY (haemophiliac male) ✓✓✓
• Phenotypes: 1/4 normal female, 1/4 carrier female, 1/4 normal male, 1/4 haemophiliac male ✓

practice.q: A pedigree shows two unaffected parents who have an affected daughter, while their sons are unaffected. Other relatives show the condition appears equally in males and females across the family. Determine, with reasons, the most likely mode of inheritance.
practice.memo:
• Most likely mode of inheritance: autosomal recessive ✓
• Reasoning: both parents are unaffected but have an affected child, meaning both parents must be heterozygous carriers ✓
• The condition affects males and females roughly equally, ruling out sex-linkage ✓
• The trait 'skipping' the parental generation is typical of recessive inheritance ✓

practice.q: Explain why a person who is heterozygous (carrier, Aa) for an autosomal recessive disorder such as cystic fibrosis does not show symptoms of the disorder.
practice.memo:
• The person has one dominant, normal allele (A) and one recessive, disease-causing allele (a) ✓
• The dominant allele is expressed and produces enough functional protein for normal function ✓
• The recessive allele's effect is masked, so the carrier shows no symptoms despite being able to pass the allele to offspring ✓

practice.q: Two parents are both heterozygous carriers (Aa) for albinism, an autosomal recessive condition. Using a Punnett square, calculate the probability that their next child will be albino, and explain your answer.
practice.memo:
• Cross: Aa × Aa ✓
• Punnett square: AA, Aa, Aa, aa ✓✓
• Probability of an albino child (aa) = 1/4 = 25% ✓

mcq.q: Evolution is best defined as ...
opts: A change in an individual organism during its lifetime
A change in the genetic composition (allele frequencies) of a population over successive generations
The growth of an organism from birth to adulthood
A single mutation in one organism
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Evolution occurs at the population level, over many generations, through changes in allele frequencies.

mcq.q: Fossils provide evidence for evolution by ...
opts: Showing living organisms in their current form only
Showing a chronological record of how organisms have changed gradually over geological time
Proving that no organisms have ever gone extinct
Disproving the existence of common ancestors
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The fossil record, especially when fossils are dated, shows transitional forms and gradual change over millions of years.

mcq.q: Homologous structures (e.g. the forelimbs of a human, whale, bat and dog, despite different functions) provide evidence for evolution because they ...
opts: Have completely different internal bone structure and no common origin
Share a similar underlying bone structure/pattern, suggesting descent from a common ancestor, even though they perform different functions
Prove organisms evolved independently with no relation
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Homologous structures reflect divergent evolution from a shared ancestral structure, adapted for different uses.

mcq.q: Analogous structures (e.g. the wings of a bird and the wings of an insect) are structures that ...
opts: Perform a similar function but evolved independently from different origins (convergent evolution), and do NOT indicate close relatedness
Always indicate a recent common ancestor
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Analogous structures arise through convergent evolution, where unrelated organisms independently evolve similar adaptations to similar environments/functions.

mcq.q: Vestigial structures (e.g. the human appendix or the pelvic bones in some whales) provide evidence for evolution because they ...
opts: Are fully functional structures essential for survival
Are reduced, seemingly non-functional remnants of structures that had a function in ancestral organisms
Prove organisms were created in their current form
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Vestigial structures suggest organisms evolved from ancestors in which the structure was functional.

mcq.q: Comparative embryology provides evidence for evolution by showing that ...
opts: Embryos of different vertebrate species look completely different from the earliest stages
Embryos of different vertebrate species (e.g. fish, chicken, human) show striking similarities in early development, suggesting a shared evolutionary ancestry
Embryonic development has no link to evolutionary history
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Early vertebrate embryos share similar structures (e.g. pharyngeal/gill slits, a tail), pointing to common ancestry.

mcq.q: Biochemical/molecular evidence (e.g. comparing DNA or protein sequences such as cytochrome c) for evolution shows that ...
opts: All species have identical DNA sequences
Species that are more closely related evolutionarily tend to show greater similarity in their DNA/protein sequences than more distantly related species
DNA comparisons are irrelevant to evolution
Only morphology can be used as evidence
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The degree of molecular similarity between species generally correlates with how recently they shared a common ancestor.

mcq.q: Biogeography (the geographic distribution of species) supports evolution by showing that ...
opts: Species are randomly distributed with no pattern
Closely related species tend to be found in geographically close or historically connected regions, reflecting their evolutionary history and dispersal
All species exist on every continent equally
Distribution has no link to ancestry
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Patterns such as unique species on isolated islands (e.g. Darwin's finches in the Galápagos) reflect evolutionary divergence from common ancestors.

mcq.q: Charles Darwin's theory of evolution by natural selection proposes that ...
opts: All individuals in a population are identical
Variation exists within a population; individuals with favourable variations are more likely to survive and reproduce, passing those traits to offspring
Evolution occurs randomly with no selective pressure
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Natural selection acts on existing heritable variation, favouring traits that improve survival and reproductive success.

mcq.q: Which of the following is a requirement for natural selection to occur in a population?
opts: Heritable variation must exist among individuals in the population
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Without heritable variation, there would be nothing for natural selection to act upon.

mcq.q: 'Survival of the fittest' in the context of natural selection refers to ...
opts: Only the physically strongest organisms surviving
Organisms best adapted to their specific environment being more likely to survive and reproduce successfully, passing on their alleles
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. 'Fitness' in evolutionary terms means reproductive success in a given environment, not necessarily physical strength.

mcq.q: A classic example of natural selection in action is the peppered moth (Biston betularia) in industrial England, where ...
opts: Dark moths became more common because pollution darkened tree bark, making dark moths better camouflaged from predators while light moths became more visible
Light moths became more common during industrial pollution
No change in moth colour occurred
mcq.exp: Correct answer: A. Soot-darkened bark gave dark (melanic) moths a survival advantage by camouflage, leading to an increase in their frequency.

mcq.q: Antibiotic resistance in bacteria develops through natural selection because ...
opts: Antibiotics cause bacteria to deliberately mutate to survive
Random mutations already present in some bacteria confer resistance; when exposed to antibiotics, resistant bacteria survive and reproduce, increasing the frequency of resistance in the population
All bacteria are equally affected by antibiotics
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The antibiotic acts as a selective pressure, favouring survival/reproduction of bacteria that already carried resistance mutations.

mcq.q: Natural selection acts directly on an organism's ...
opts: Genotype only, without any effect from phenotype
Phenotype, which in turn determines reproductive success and indirectly affects which alleles (genotype) are passed to the next generation
Behaviour exclusively, never on physical traits
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Selection pressures act on observable, expressed traits (phenotype); this determines survival/reproduction, which then influences allele frequencies in the next generation.

mcq.q: Genetic variation, the raw material for natural selection, primarily arises from ...
opts: Identical replication of DNA with no errors
Mutation, and (in sexually reproducing organisms) meiosis (crossing over, independent assortment) and random fertilisation
Environmental change alone, without any genetic basis
The deliberate effort of organisms to change
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Mutation introduces new alleles; sexual reproduction reshuffles existing alleles into new combinations.

mcq.q: A mutation is best defined as ...
opts: A normal process of cell division
A random, heritable change in the DNA sequence of an organism
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Mutations can be neutral, harmful, or occasionally beneficial, providing new genetic variation for selection to act upon.
