mcq.q: Genetic drift differs from natural selection in that genetic drift ...
opts: Causes random changes in allele frequencies, especially in SMALL populations, regardless of whether the alleles are beneficial, neutral or harmful
Is the same process as natural selection
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Genetic drift is a random, non-selective process that has a particularly strong effect in small populations.

mcq.q: The bottleneck effect is a type of genetic drift that occurs when ...
opts: A population increases dramatically in size
A population's size is drastically and suddenly reduced (e.g. by a natural disaster), leaving a small, often non-representative, surviving gene pool
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The surviving population's allele frequencies may differ greatly (by chance) from the original population, reducing genetic diversity.

mcq.q: The founder effect occurs when ...
opts: An entire established population suddenly changes

A small group of individuals becomes isolated from a larger population (e.g. colonising a new island) and establishes a new population with allele frequencies that may differ from the original population purely by chance
Natural selection acts with maximum intensity
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Because the founding group is small and may not represent the full genetic diversity of the original population, allele frequencies can differ significantly.

mcq.q: Speciation is the process by which ...
opts: A single individual changes during its lifetime
New, distinct species arise from an ancestral population, usually through accumulated genetic divergence over time
Organisms within a species interbreed freely
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Speciation typically requires populations to become reproductively isolated from each other long enough to accumulate sufficient genetic differences.

mcq.q: A biological species is generally defined as a group of organisms that ...
opts: Live in the exact same location
Can interbreed and produce fertile offspring under natural conditions
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Reproductive compatibility (producing fertile offspring) is the key criterion of the biological species concept.

mcq.q: Allopatric speciation occurs when populations become separated by ...
opts: No barrier at all, while remaining in the same area
A geographic barrier (e.g. a mountain range, river or ocean), preventing gene flow and allowing the populations to diverge genetically over time
Behavioural differences only, with no physical separation
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Geographic isolation is the most common driver of speciation, preventing interbreeding and allowing independent evolutionary paths.

mcq.q: Sympatric speciation occurs when new species arise ...
opts: Within the same geographic area, without physical separation, often due to other reproductive isolating mechanisms (e.g. behavioural or temporal differences, polyploidy in plants)
Through identical environments with full gene flow maintained
Exclusively in animals, never in plants
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Sympatric speciation can occur via mechanisms such as differences in breeding time, habitat preference within the same region, or polyploidy.

mcq.q: Reproductive isolation refers to ...
opts: Any mechanism that prevents members of different populations/species from successfully interbreeding and producing fertile offspring
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Reproductive isolating mechanisms (geographic, behavioural, temporal, mechanical, genetic) maintain species as distinct.

mcq.q: Adaptive radiation refers to ...
opts: The extinction of a single species
The relatively rapid evolution of many new species from a single ancestral species, each adapted to a different ecological niche (e.g. Darwin's finches)
A decrease in biodiversity over time
The fusion of two species into one
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Adaptive radiation often occurs when a species colonises a new environment with diverse, unoccupied niches.

mcq.q: Darwin's finches on the Galápagos Islands, showing a variety of beak shapes adapted to different food sources, are a classic example of ...
opts: Adaptive radiation from a common ancestral finch species
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Different islands/niches favoured different beak shapes, leading to diversification from a shared ancestor.

mcq.q: Humans (Homo sapiens) belong to the order ... and family ...
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Humans are classified within the primate order and the great ape family Hominidae.

mcq.q: Bipedalism (walking upright on two legs) is considered an important early adaptation in human evolution because it ...
opts: Freed the hands for tool use and carrying objects, and may have improved energy efficiency for long-distance walking
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Bipedalism is one of the earliest distinguishing hominin traits, predating significant increases in brain size.

mcq.q: Australopithecus is a genus of early hominin generally characterised by ...
opts: A fully modern, large brain and advanced tool use
Bipedal walking but a relatively small, ape-like brain
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Australopithecus species (e.g. Australopithecus afarensis, 'Lucy') show clear adaptations for bipedalism, but brain size remained closer to that of apes.

mcq.q: The genus Homo is generally distinguished from Australopithecus by ...
opts: A smaller brain and exclusively quadrupedal movement
A larger brain size, more advanced tool use, and eventually more modern body proportions
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. Species of Homo (e.g. Homo habilis, Homo erectus, Homo sapiens) show a general trend of increasing brain size and cultural/technological complexity over time.

mcq.q: Homo sapiens is generally believed, based on fossil and genetic evidence, to have originated in ...
opts: Africa, before later migrating to other continents
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The 'Out of Africa' model is strongly supported by fossil evidence and genetic studies (e.g. mitochondrial DNA analysis).

mcq.q: Fossil evidence for human evolution shows a general trend, over millions of years, of ...
opts: Decreasing brain size and increasing jaw/tooth size
Increasing brain size, a more upright posture, and reduced jaw/tooth size relative to earlier hominins
No change in any physical features
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. The hominin fossil record generally shows these trends as species became more anatomically modern.

mcq.q: The use of comparative genomics, comparing human DNA to that of chimpanzees, shows that humans and chimpanzees share approximately ... of their DNA, supporting a relatively recent common ancestor.
mcq.exp: Correct answer: B. This very high genetic similarity supports the close evolutionary relationship between humans and chimpanzees, our closest living relatives.

practice.q: Distinguish between homologous and analogous structures, using an example of each.
practice.memo:
• Homologous structures: similar underlying structure due to common ancestry, but may have different functions, e.g. forelimbs of humans, whales and bats ✓✓
• Analogous structures: similar function but different evolutionary origin (convergent evolution), e.g. the wings of birds and insects ✓✓

practice.q: Explain how the fossil record provides evidence in support of the theory of evolution.
practice.memo:
• Fossils show a chronological sequence of organisms found in successive rock layers ✓
• Older rock layers generally contain simpler/more primitive organisms, while younger layers show more complex forms ✓
• Transitional fossils show intermediate features between ancestral and modern forms ✓
• This pattern supports gradual change of species over geological time ✓

practice.q: Using the peppered moth as an example, explain how natural selection led to a change in the proportion of dark and light moths during the Industrial Revolution.
practice.memo:
• Before industrial pollution, light-coloured moths were well camouflaged on lichen-covered, light tree bark, while dark moths were easily seen and eaten by predators (birds) ✓✓
• Industrial pollution killed lichen and darkened tree bark with soot ✓
• Dark moths became better camouflaged, while light moths became more visible/vulnerable to predation ✓✓
• Dark moths therefore survived and reproduced more successfully, passing on the allele for dark colouration ✓
• Over generations, the frequency of dark moths in the population increased ✓

practice.q: List FOUR requirements/conditions necessary for natural selection to occur within a population.
practice.memo:
• Variation must exist among individuals in the population ✓
• The variation must be heritable (genetically based) ✓
• Resources (e.g. food, space) must be limited, leading to competition/a struggle for survival ✓
• Individuals with favourable variations must have a greater chance of survival and reproduction (differential reproductive success) ✓

practice.q: Explain THREE sources of genetic variation within a sexually reproducing population.
practice.memo:
• Mutation: random changes in DNA sequence create new alleles ✓✓
• Crossing over during meiosis: exchange of genetic material between homologous chromosomes creates new allele combinations ✓✓
• Independent assortment during meiosis and random fertilisation: random combination of maternal/paternal chromosomes and random fusion of gametes increases variation among offspring ✓✓

practice.q: Distinguish between the bottleneck effect and the founder effect as examples of genetic drift.
practice.memo:
• Bottleneck effect: a population's size is drastically reduced (e.g. by a disaster), leaving a small surviving population whose allele frequencies may not represent the original population ✓✓
• Founder effect: a small group breaks away/becomes isolated from a larger population to found a new population, which may have different allele frequencies purely by chance, due to the small founding sample ✓✓

practice.q: Using an example, explain how allopatric speciation can lead to the formation of a new species.
practice.memo:
• A geographic barrier (e.g. a river, mountain range, or ocean) separates a population into two groups ✓
• Gene flow between the two groups is prevented ✓
• Each group experiences different environmental conditions/selective pressures and accumulates different mutations over time ✓✓
• Over many generations, the two populations become genetically increasingly different ✓
• Eventually they may become so different that they can no longer interbreed successfully (reproductively isolated) even if reunited, forming two distinct species ✓

practice.q: Explain, with reference to Darwin's finches, what is meant by adaptive radiation.
practice.memo:
• Adaptive radiation: the relatively rapid evolution of multiple new species from a single common ancestor ✓
• Each new species becomes adapted to a different ecological niche/food source ✓
• In Darwin's finches, an ancestral finch species colonised the Galápagos Islands and diversified into many species, each with a beak shape suited to a particular food source available on different islands ✓✓

practice.q: Explain why bipedalism is considered a key early adaptation in hominin evolution, even before significant increases in brain size occurred.
practice.memo:
• Bipedalism freed the hands, which could then be used for carrying objects, food, or offspring, and later for tool use/manipulation ✓✓
• It may have improved energy efficiency for covering long distances on the ground, useful as forests gave way to more open savanna habitats ✓✓
• Fossil evidence (e.g. pelvis and leg bone structure) shows bipedalism evolved long before substantial brain enlargement, indicating it was not initially driven by increased intelligence ✓

practice.q: Describe the general evolutionary trends observed in the hominin fossil record from early Australopithecus species to modern Homo sapiens.
practice.memo:
• Increasing brain size/cranial capacity over time ✓✓
• Progressive change towards a more fully upright, bipedal posture and modern skeletal proportions ✓✓
• Reduction in the size of the jaw and teeth ✓
• Increasing complexity of tool use and other cultural/technological behaviour ✓
